questions.q: Explain the four pillars of OOP.

answer.oneLiner: So the four pillars are Encapsulation, Inheritance, Polymorphism, and Abstraction. The easiest way to picture them is as four habits that good object-oriented code follows: you protect your data so nothing outside messes it up, you reuse code by inheriting from a parent class, you let the same method call do different things depending on which object is actually behind it, and you hide all the messy details behind a simple, clean interface. Put together, they're what make your code easy to grow, test, and keep tidy over time.
keyPoints: Encapsulation is about bundling your data and the methods that work on it together, and only exposing what people actually need through access modifiers, so outside code can't reach in and corrupt the internal state.

Inheritance lets a child class reuse and build on a parent class, which is basically an IS-A relationship, so a Dog is an Animal and automatically gets all the Animal behaviour without you rewriting it.

Polymorphism is when the same method call behaves differently depending on the actual type at runtime, and in C# you get that with the virtual and override keywords so the right method runs for the right object.

Abstraction is about hiding the implementation details behind a clean contract, usually an abstract class or an interface, so the caller only sees what they care about and isn't tied to how it works under the hood.

code.note: All four pillars in one small example

answer.analogy: A car: pedals are the abstraction, engine internals are encapsulated, a Tesla overrides propulsion (polymorphism).
redFlags: Confusing abstraction with encapsulation.
Saying polymorphism is only overloading.
No concrete code example.

dialogue.text: Can you walk me through the four pillars of OOP?

Sure - **encapsulation** hides internal state, **inheritance** reuses a base via IS-A, **polymorphism** lets the same call behave differently per type, and **abstraction** exposes only the essentials behind a contract.
Encapsulation and abstraction sound similar - how do you separate them?
Abstraction is about *what* you expose (the clean interface); encapsulation is about *how* you protect the data behind it with access modifiers.
Give me a concrete example of polymorphism.
`Animal a = new Dog(); a.Speak();` dispatches to `Dog.Speak()` at runtime because `Speak` is `virtual` and overridden.

followUpsQA.a: Overloading is compile-time; virtual/override is runtime dispatch.
When behavior varies and you want loose coupling.
No - overloading is compile-time polymorphism too.

questions.q: Explain the SOLID principles with a brief example for each.

answer.oneLiner: SOLID is just five design principles that nudge you toward code that's easy to extend, test, and maintain. In plain terms: Single Responsibility says each class should do one job; Open/Closed says you add new behaviour by writing new code instead of editing code that already works; Liskov Substitution says a subtype has to work anywhere its base type is expected without surprises; Interface Segregation says lots of small focused interfaces beat one giant do-everything one; and Dependency Inversion says your high-level code should depend on abstractions, not on specific concrete classes.
keyPoints: Single Responsibility means a class should only have one reason to change, so for example the thing that calculates a report and the thing that prints it should be two separate classes.
Open/Closed means you should be able to add new behaviour by writing a new class or implementation, rather than going back and editing code that's already working.

Liskov Substitution means a subclass has to be a drop-in replacement for its base class without breaking callers, so a Square that extends Rectangle and quietly changes the width when you set the height is a classic violation.
Interface Segregation means you should favour several small, focused interfaces over one huge interface, so a class only has to implement the bits it actually uses.

Dependency Inversion means high-level code should depend on an abstraction like IRepository instead of a concrete class like SqlRepository, which makes the code testable and lets you swap implementations easily.

code.note: DIP via constructor injection — the most common pattern in ASP.NET Core

redFlags: Reciting the acronym with no examples.
Confusing OCP with 'never modify code'.
Not mentioning testability as the DIP payoff.

dialogue.text: Walk me through SOLID.
**S**ingle responsibility, **O**pen/closed, **L**iskov substitution, **I**nterface segregation, **D**ependency inversion - five principles for maintainable, testable code.
Pick one and show why it matters.
DIP - depend on `IRepository`, not `SqlRepository`. That lets me inject a fake in tests and swap implementations without touching the consumer.
Can you over-apply OCP?
Yes - adding abstractions for changes that never happen is speculative generality; apply it once a real second variation appears.

followUpsQA.q: Which SOLID principle does ASP.NET Core DI enable?

followUpsQA.a: A `Square : Rectangle` where setting width also changes height breaks callers.
Many small focused interfaces beat one fat interface.

questions.q: What is the difference between an abstract class and an interface, and when do you use each?

answer.oneLiner: An abstract class is a base class that's partly filled in already, so it can hold shared state and real working methods, and it models an IS-A relationship where all the subtypes have common behaviour. An interface, on the other hand, is just a pure capability contract with no state at all, and a class can implement as many interfaces as it wants. I reach for an abstract class when the subclasses genuinely share code or fields, and I reach for an interface when I just need a capability that unrelated types can opt into, like IDisposable.
keyPoints: An abstract class can have fields, constructors, and fully implemented methods, which makes it great for sharing code across a class hierarchy using something like the Template Method pattern.
A class can only inherit from one base class, but it can implement as many interfaces as it needs, and that's really the main practical difference between the two.
From C# 8 onwards you can put default implementations in interfaces, but interfaces still can't hold instance state, so the distinction between the two hasn't gone away.
Interfaces are usually easier to mock in unit tests because the frameworks can just generate a stub implementation automatically, without you having to subclass anything.
answer.detail: Choose abstract class when subclasses genuinely share code/state (Template Method). Choose interface for capabilities across unrelated types (`IDisposable`) and for stable public library contracts.

code.note: Template Method pattern via abstract class + IExportable capability via interface

answer.analogy: Abstract class = job description with shared office/HR; interface = a certification many different people can hold.
redFlags: Saying 'interfaces have no implementation' (ignores C# 8 DIM).
Not mentioning multiple interface implementation.
No real-world scenario for each.

dialogue.text: Abstract class vs interface - when do you reach for each?
Abstract class when subclasses share state or code (IS-A, Template Method); interface for a capability contract across unrelated types, like `IDisposable`.
C# 8 added default interface methods - does that blur the line?
A bit, but interfaces still can't hold instance state and you can implement many of them, so I keep interfaces stateless and use abstract classes when shared fields or ctors are needed.
Which is safer to change in a shipped library?
Abstract classes - adding a member to a public interface breaks every implementer, whereas an abstract class can add a non-abstract method without breaking subclasses.

followUpsQA.a: Default bodies so you can add interface members without breaking implementers.

questions.q: What is Dependency Injection and how is it implemented in ASP.NET Core?

answer.oneLiner: Dependency Injection just means a class gets handed the objects it needs from the outside, instead of creating them itself with new. ASP.NET Core ships with a built-in DI container where you register your services in Program.cs, and then the framework automatically figures out what's needed and injects it through the constructor. It's really the practical, everyday way of applying the Dependency Inversion principle.
keyPoints: There are three service lifetimes: Transient gives you a brand new instance every single time you ask for it, Scoped gives you one instance per HTTP request which is perfect for something like DbContext, and Singleton gives you one instance for the whole lifetime of the app.
You register your services in Program.cs using builder.Services.AddScoped, AddTransient, or AddSingleton, which maps an abstraction like IOrderService to a concrete implementation like OrderService.

Constructor injection is the way you normally want to do it: you just declare your dependencies as constructor parameters and the container resolves and wires up the whole object graph for you automatically.

Watch out for the captive dependency problem, where a Singleton takes a Scoped service as a dependency, because then that Scoped instance gets kept alive for the entire app lifetime instead of being disposed each request, and ASP.NET Core will actually throw an exception in Development mode if it spots this.
answer.detail: `Scoped` is ideal for `DbContext`. A Singleton holding a Scoped service keeps it alive for the whole app — ASP.NET Core throws on this in Development.

code.note: Constructor injection — the container resolves IOrderRepository automatically

redFlags: Confusing DI (pattern) with the container (tool).
Not knowing the three lifetimes.
Using service locator everywhere instead of injection.

dialogue.text: What is dependency injection and how does ASP.NET Core do it?

DI supplies a class's dependencies from outside instead of `new`-ing them inside. ASP.NET Core has a built-in container - you register services in `Program.cs` via `builder.Services` and it resolves constructors.
What are the lifetimes?
`Transient` (new every time), `Scoped` (one per request - good for `DbContext`), and `Singleton` (one for the app).
What's a captive dependency?
A Singleton that holds a Scoped service - it keeps that scoped instance alive for the whole app. ASP.NET Core throws on this in Development.

followUpsQA.a: To create a scope manually in a singleton or background service.
Prefer constructor injection; service locator hides dependencies.

questions.q: What is the difference between method overloading and method overriding?

answer.oneLiner: Overloading is when you have several methods with the same name but different parameters, and the compiler picks which one to call at compile time based on the argument types. Overriding is when a subclass swaps out a virtual base method with its own version, and the right one gets chosen at runtime based on the actual object you've got. The simplest way to remember it is that overloading is the compiler deciding, and overriding is the runtime deciding.
keyPoints: Overloading is sorted out at compile time by matching your argument types to the available method signatures, so the right method is basically baked into the compiled code.
Overriding needs the base method to be marked virtual or abstract, and the derived class to use the override keyword, and then the dispatch happens at runtime through the virtual method table.
You can't tell overloads apart just by their return type, the parameter list itself has to be different.

If you use the new keyword instead of override, you're hiding the base method rather than overriding it, which means a base-typed reference will still call the original, and that quietly breaks polymorphism and is usually a design mistake.

code.note: Overloading = compile-time; overriding = runtime dispatch

redFlags: Saying they're the same thing.
Not knowing overriding requires `virtual` on the base.

dialogue.text: Overloading vs overriding?
Overloading is same name, different signatures, resolved at **compile time**. Overriding replaces a `virtual` base method in a subclass, resolved at **runtime**.
What does overriding require on the base?
The base method must be `virtual` or `abstract`, and the derived uses `override`.
What if I use `new` instead of `override`?
That *hides* the base method - a base-typed reference still calls the original, so you lose polymorphism. Usually a smell.

followUpsQA.q: Can you override a static method?

followUpsQA.a: No - overloads must differ by parameters.
Shadowing the base member; resolution depends on the declared type.

questions.q: Explain `virtual`, `override`, `new`, and `sealed` in C# inheritance.

answer.oneLiner: These four keywords are how you control inheritance in C#. Virtual marks a method as one that can be overridden; override replaces it in a derived class with full polymorphic dispatch; new hides a method without polymorphism so a base-typed reference still calls the original; and sealed either stops a class from being inherited at all, or stops a particular override from being overridden any further down the chain.
keyPoints: Virtual is what opts a method into runtime dispatch, and unlike Java, C# methods are non-virtual by default, so you have to explicitly mark them virtual before anyone can override them.
Override replaces the virtual method's slot in the virtual method table, so any call through a base reference ends up dispatching to the most-derived override at runtime.

New hides the base method instead of overriding it, so if you're holding a reference of the base type, the original method gets called rather than your new version, and that's usually a sign something's off with the design.

Sealed on a class stops anyone inheriting from it further, and sealed on an override stops any deeper subclass from overriding that method again, and as a bonus it lets the JIT devirtualize the call for a small performance win.

code.note: Without `virtual`+`override`, `b.Greet()` would print "Base" regardless of runtime type

redFlags: Not knowing C# methods are non-virtual by default.
Confusing `new` (hiding) with `override`.

dialogue.text: Explain `virtual`, `override`, `new`, and `sealed`.
`virtual` opts a method into runtime dispatch, `override` replaces it in a subclass, `new` hides it without polymorphism, and `sealed` stops further overriding or inheritance.
Are C# methods virtual by default?
No - non-virtual by default, unlike Java. You opt in with `virtual`.
What does a base reference call when the derived used `new`?
The base version - `new` resolves by the declared type, not the runtime type.

followUpsQA.a: To stop further overriding and let the JIT devirtualize.

followUpsQA.q: new vs override on a base reference?
followUpsQA.a: new calls base; override calls derived.
No - the base must be virtual or abstract.

questions.q: What are access modifiers in C# and what does each allow?

answer.oneLiner: C# has six access modifiers that control who's allowed to see a given member or type. Public means anyone can see it, private locks it down to just the class that declares it, protected opens it up to derived classes, internal limits it to the same assembly, protected internal is the union of protected and internal, and private protected is the intersection, meaning only derived classes that live in the same assembly.
keyPoints: Public means the member is visible from anywhere, while private means only the declaring class itself can touch it, and private is actually the default for class members.
Protected lets the declaring class and any of its derived classes get at the member, no matter what assembly they're in.
Internal limits access to code in the same assembly, and it's the default visibility for top-level types.
Protected internal is more open than either one on its own, because it's accessible from the same assembly OR from any derived class in any assembly.
Private protected is the strictest combination of the two, because only derived classes that also live in the same assembly are allowed to access the member.
redFlags: Not knowing `internal`.
Forgetting `[InternalsVisibleTo]` for test assemblies.

dialogue.text: Name the C# access modifiers.
`public`, `private`, `protected`, `internal`, `protected internal`, and `private protected`.
What are the defaults?
Class members default to `private`; top-level types default to `internal`.
How do you let a test project see internal members?
Add `[assembly: InternalsVisibleTo("MyTests")]` so the test assembly can access internals.

followUpsQA.a: Same assembly OR any derived class.
Derived classes within the same assembly only.

questions.q: What is the difference between static and instance members?

answer.oneLiner: Static members belong to the type itself rather than to any one object, so they're shared across every instance of the class and they stick around for the whole life of the application. Instance members belong to each individual object and each one keeps its own separate state. I usually describe static members as the company-wide policy, and instance members as each employee's own personal desk.
keyPoints: You access static members through the type name rather than through an object reference, and they exist even when you haven't created a single instance yet.
Static fields hold state that's shared across all instances, which means you have to be careful about thread safety when several threads might be reading and writing them at the same time.
A static class can't be instantiated and can only hold static members, which makes it a natural home for utility and helper methods, like the ones on the Math class.
Good places to use static members are pure utility methods, constants, extension methods, and factory methods, basically anything that doesn't depend on the state of a particular object.

code.note: `_total` is shared across all Counter instances

redFlags: Overusing static state — hard to test, not thread-safe.
Not knowing static constructors run once, lazily.

dialogue.text: Static vs instance members?
Static members belong to the type and are shared across all instances; instance members hold per-object state.
When do static members bite you?
Shared static mutable state isn't thread-safe and makes unit tests interfere with each other - it's global state in disguise.
When do static constructors run?
Once per type, lazily, just before first use - and the runtime makes it thread-safe.

followUpsQA.q: Can a static class be instantiated?

followUpsQA.a: Not thread-safe and hard to test.

questions.q: What is 'composition over inheritance' and why is it preferred?

answer.oneLiner: Composition over inheritance means you build up behaviour by combining small, focused objects that your class holds onto as dependencies, instead of building these deep inheritance trees. It ends up giving you more flexible, testable, loosely coupled code, because you can swap collaborators out at runtime and change behaviour without ever touching the class hierarchy.
keyPoints: Inheritance ties a subclass tightly to its parent's internals, so any change to the base class can break all the subclasses in ways that are really hard to predict.
Composition lets you inject different collaborators at runtime or in your tests, so you can change behaviour without having to touch the class structure at all.
Deep inheritance hierarchies are fragile because a single change to a base class ripples down through every level, and on top of that you're limited to just one base class in C#.
I'd only reach for inheritance when there's a stable, genuine IS-A relationship and you actually need a shared algorithm structure, like with the Template Method pattern.

code.note: Injecting strategy objects avoids deep inheritance chains

dialogue.text: What's 'composition over inheritance'?
Build behavior by combining small objects (HAS-A) - inject collaborators - instead of deep class hierarchies. More flexible and loosely coupled.
Why is deep inheritance fragile?
Subclasses depend on base internals, so a base change can break all of them, and you can only inherit one base.
When is inheritance still right?
When there's a stable, genuine IS-A relationship and you want a shared algorithm skeleton, like Template Method.

followUpsQA.a: ASP.NET Core middleware composes behavior from small components.
DI is composition - you inject collaborators rather than inherit.
Composition is HAS-A; inheritance is IS-A.

questions.q: What is runtime polymorphism vs compile-time polymorphism in C#?

answer.oneLiner: Compile-time polymorphism is when the compiler works out which method to call based on the argument types before the program even runs, which is what you get with method overloading and operator overloading. Runtime polymorphism is when the CLR decides which implementation to call while the program is running, based on the actual type of the object, which is what happens with virtual and override. The compile-time kind is faster but less flexible, and the runtime kind is what makes patterns like Strategy and Template Method possible.
keyPoints: Compile-time polymorphism covers method overloading, operator overloading, and generics, and all of those get resolved by the compiler before the program ever runs.

Runtime polymorphism works through the virtual method table, where each object carries a pointer to its type's method table, and a virtual call looks up the right slot at runtime and dispatches to the most-derived override.
C# generics get specialised per value type by the JIT instead of being erased like Java's, so a List of int actually uses an int array with no boxing and better performance.
Runtime polymorphism is exactly what makes the Strategy and Template Method patterns work, because the correct behaviour is picked based on the real object rather than the type you declared.

dialogue.text: Runtime vs compile-time polymorphism?
Compile-time is overloading and operators - the compiler picks the method. Runtime is `virtual`/`override` and interface dispatch via the vtable, resolved by the object's actual type.
How does the CLR do virtual dispatch?
Each object has a method-table pointer; a virtual call looks up the slot at runtime, so the most-derived override runs.
Why are C# generics faster than Java's for value types?
C# reifies generics and specializes per value type, so `List<int>` uses `int[]` with no boxing; Java erases the type and boxes.

followUpsQA.a: Yes - parametric polymorphism, resolved at compile time.

questions.q: What does the `sealed` keyword do and when should you use it?

answer.oneLiner: The sealed keyword tells C# that a class can't be inherited from, or that an overridden method can't be overridden any further in subclasses. A well-known example is the string class in .NET, which is sealed. Beyond just expressing your design intent, sealing a class or method also lets the JIT compiler devirtualize the method calls, which can give you a small but real performance bump.
keyPoints: A sealed class can't be used as a base class, so nobody can build an inheritance hierarchy on top of it, which protects its invariants and signals that extension was never part of the plan.
Putting sealed on an override stops the override chain right there, so no deeper subclass can replace that method again.
The JIT compiler can devirtualize and maybe even inline calls to sealed members because it knows no further override is possible, which gets rid of the virtual dispatch overhead.

Use sealed to make it clear that a class isn't meant to be extended, which is especially handy for immutable, value-like types where subclassing could undermine your equality or thread-safety guarantees.

dialogue.text: What does `sealed` do?
On a class it blocks inheritance; on an override it stops further overriding. `string` is sealed, for example.
Any performance angle?
Yes - the JIT can devirtualize and inline calls to sealed members since no further override is possible.
When would you seal a class?
When it's not designed to be extended - to protect invariants and signal intent, like immutable value types.

followUpsQA.a: An override that can't be overridden again.

questions.q: What is the difference between value types and reference types in C#?

answer.oneLiner: Value types store their actual data right inside the variable, so when you assign one to another you get a completely independent copy. Reference types store a reference that points to an object on the heap, so assigning one to another just gives you two variables pointing at the same object. This one distinction is behind nearly every mutation bug and every bit of equality confusion you'll hit in C#.
keyPoints: When you assign a value type, all the data gets copied directly, so the two variables are completely independent and changing one has no effect on the other.
When you assign a reference type, only the reference pointer gets copied, so both variables point at the same heap object and a change made through one is visible through the other.

A value-type field that sits inside a class actually lives on the heap right alongside the object that contains it, not on the stack, so the common shorthand that value types always live on the stack is really an oversimplification.

Examples of value types are int, bool, struct, and DateTime, while examples of reference types are string, arrays, and any class, though string is a special reference type that acts like a value type because it's immutable and overrides equality to compare the actual content.
answer.detail: 'Value types on stack' is a simplification: a value-type field inside a class lives on the heap with that object. The real distinction is copy semantics vs shared references, which drives mutation bugs and equality behavior.

code.note: Assignment behavior is the most practical difference

answer.analogy: Value type = a photocopy; reference type = a shared Google Doc link.
redFlags: 'Value types are always on the stack' with no nuance.
Not knowing strings are reference types with value equality.

dialogue.text: Value types vs reference types?
Value types hold their data directly and copy on assignment; reference types hold a pointer to a heap object, so assignment copies the reference and both names share one object.
I often hear 'value types live on the stack' - is that accurate?
It's a simplification. A value-type *field* inside a class lives on the heap with that object. The real distinction is copy semantics versus shared references.
Strings are reference types but feel like values - why?
`string` is a reference type but immutable with value-based equality, so `==` compares contents, which makes it behave value-like.

followUpsQA.q: Where is a value-type field inside a class allocated?
followUpsQA.a: On the heap, inside the containing object.

followUpsQA.q: How do records differ from structs on equality?
followUpsQA.a: Both give value equality; record class is still a reference type.
struct is a value type, class a reference type.
Copies cause silent lost updates, especially in collections.

questions.q: What is boxing and unboxing? Why is it a performance concern?

answer.oneLiner: Boxing is when a value type gets wrapped up in a heap-allocated object so it can be treated as a reference type, and unboxing is the reverse, where you cast it back to pull the value out. Both of those involve a heap allocation and a copy of the data, so if you keep doing it in a hot path it piles on garbage collection pressure and slows things down. The fix is almost always to use generics instead of object or the old non-generic collections.
keyPoints: Every time you box something, it allocates a brand new heap object to wrap the value, which puts pressure on the garbage collector and hurts performance when it's happening a lot.
Unboxing needs an explicit cast back to the original value type, and if you cast to the wrong type it'll throw an InvalidCastException at runtime.

Common ways you accidentally box things include passing value types to methods that take an object parameter, using non-generic collections like ArrayList, and calling string.Format with value type arguments.
The standard fix is to use generic collections and methods, for instance List of int instead of ArrayList, so the value type never gets wrapped in a heap object in the first place.

code.note: List<T> uses a generic array internally — zero boxing

redFlags: Not knowing `ArrayList` boxes everything.
Claiming `$"Hello {x}"` never boxes.

dialogue.text: What is boxing and unboxing?
Boxing wraps a value type in a heap `object`; unboxing casts it back. Each box is a heap allocation plus a copy.
Why is it a performance concern?
In hot paths it creates GC pressure - non-generic collections like `ArrayList` box every element. Switching to `List<int>` removes it entirely.
Is boxing ever unavoidable?
Mostly no with generics, but anything that takes `object` - like a non-generic API or certain interface calls on structs - can still box.

followUpsQA.a: Yes - the int is passed as `object`.

questions.q: How does the .NET garbage collector work? Explain generations.

answer.oneLiner: The .NET garbage collector is a generational mark-and-compact collector, and it's built around the observation that most objects die young. It splits the heap into three generations: Gen 0 for freshly created objects, Gen 1 as a short-lived survivor buffer, and Gen 2 for the long-lived stuff. Gen 0 gets collected really often and cheaply, whereas a full Gen 2 collection is expensive and you want it to be rare.
keyPoints: Gen 0 holds the newly allocated objects and gets collected very frequently, and most objects are freed right here before they ever get promoted, which is why Gen 0 collections are so fast.
Gen 1 acts as a buffer sitting between Gen 0 and Gen 2, so objects that survive a Gen 0 collection move up to Gen 1, and objects that survive Gen 1 move up to Gen 2.

Gen 2 holds the long-lived objects, and a full Gen 2 collection is the most expensive thing because it scans the whole heap, so you want to keep it infrequent by avoiding lots of long-lived allocations.
The Large Object Heap holds objects that are 85 KB or bigger, and it gets collected along with Gen 2 but isn't compacted by default, which can lead to fragmentation over time.
An object stays alive for as long as it's reachable from a GC root, which includes things like static fields, local variables on the stack, and GC handles.
answer.detail: GC is triggered by allocation pressure, not a timer. Finalizers delay collection by one cycle, so prefer `Dispose` + `GC.SuppressFinalize`. For throughput use Server GC and pool buffers with `ArrayPool<T>`/`Span<T>`.
redFlags: Thinking GC is timer-triggered.
Not knowing finalizers delay collection.

dialogue.text: How does the .NET garbage collector work?
It's a generational mark-and-compact collector. Gen 0 is new objects collected often, Gen 1 is a buffer, Gen 2 is long-lived. It exploits 'most objects die young'.
What triggers a collection?
Allocation pressure, not a timer - when a generation's budget is exceeded. Gen 0 collections are cheap and frequent; full Gen 2 collections are expensive.
How do finalizers affect this?
A finalizable object survives an extra GC cycle, so prefer `Dispose` plus `GC.SuppressFinalize`. Also, objects >=85KB go on the LOH, collected with Gen 2.

followUpsQA.a: Server uses per-core heaps for throughput; Workstation is low-latency single heap.
It removes the object from the finalization queue, avoiding an extra cycle.

questions.q: What is the difference between `struct` and `class` in C#?

answer.oneLiner: A struct is a value type that stores its data directly and copies all of it on assignment, which makes it a good fit for small, immutable data like coordinates or colours. A class is a reference type that lives on the heap and only copies the reference on assignment, and it gives you the full set of object-oriented features like inheritance. My rule of thumb is to use a struct when the type is small, around 16 bytes or less, logically immutable, and value equality actually makes sense for it.
keyPoints: A struct copies all of its fields when you assign it, so you end up with two completely independent values, whereas assigning a class only copies the reference so both variables point at the same heap object.
A struct can't inherit from another struct or class but it can implement interfaces, and it doesn't get default reference equality, so you usually have to override Equals and GetHashCode yourself.
Use a struct for small, immutable, frequently-created data like a Point or a Color, to avoid heap allocations and the garbage collection pressure that comes with them.

Steer clear of large or mutable structs, because large ones are expensive to copy on every method call, and mutable ones sitting in collections will quietly mutate a copy instead of the original, which causes really hard-to-find bugs.

C# 10 added parameterless constructors for structs, and the record struct keyword gives you all the struct performance benefits along with auto-generated equality, ToString, and with-expression support.

code.note: Use `record struct` in C# 10+ for immutable value types with value equality

redFlags: Recommending structs for large objects.
Not knowing mutable structs in collections cause silent bugs.

dialogue.text: struct vs class?
`struct` is a value type with copy semantics and no inheritance; `class` is a reference type with full OOP and heap allocation.
When would you actually choose a struct?
For small, immutable, frequently-created data - roughly 16 bytes or less, like a `Point` - to avoid heap allocations.
What's the danger with large or mutable structs?
Large structs are expensive to copy, and mutable structs in collections mutate a *copy*, causing silent bugs. I keep structs small and immutable.

followUpsQA.a: When the struct is immutable - it prevents defensive copies.
Yes, but not inherit a class.

questions.q: What is the difference between `IEnumerable<T>` and `IQueryable<T>`?

answer.oneLiner: IEnumerable of T iterates over data that's already in memory using C# delegate-based LINQ, while IQueryable of T builds up an expression tree that a query provider like EF Core turns into SQL before any data is even fetched. So if you filter on an IQueryable, the WHERE clause runs in the database, but if you call ToList first and then filter, you've pulled the whole table into memory and you're filtering in C#, which is usually a serious performance mistake.
keyPoints: IEnumerable of T runs its LINQ operators as in-memory C# delegates, so every operation works on data that's already been loaded into the application process.
IQueryable of T captures its LINQ operators as an expression tree, which a query provider like EF Core can look at and translate into a SQL query that runs over in the database.
Calling ToList materialises the query and flips you from database execution to in-memory execution, so any LINQ operators you tack on after ToList will run in C# rather than in SQL.
Always do your filtering, ordering, and projection on the IQueryable before you materialise it, so the database does the heavy lifting and only the rows you actually need come across the network.

code.note: Always filter on IQueryable before materializing

redFlags: Putting `.ToList()` early 'just to be safe'.
Not knowing IQueryable carries an expression tree.

dialogue.text: IEnumerable vs IQueryable?
`IEnumerable<T>` runs LINQ in memory with delegates; `IQueryable<T>` builds an expression tree that EF Core translates to SQL.
Why does the difference matter in practice?
If you call `.ToList()` before filtering, you pull the whole table into memory then filter. Filtering on `IQueryable` keeps the WHERE in SQL.
When would you intentionally switch to IEnumerable?
When a `Where` uses a method EF can't translate - I materialize with `AsEnumerable()` first, accepting the in-memory cost.

followUpsQA.q: What happens with an untranslatable method?
followUpsQA.a: EF throws or you must evaluate client-side.

questions.q: Explain the `IDisposable` interface and the `using` statement.

answer.oneLiner: IDisposable is an interface with a single Dispose method, and you implement it when your type holds resources that need to be cleaned up at a definite moment rather than whenever the garbage collector eventually gets around to it. The using statement is just a language convenience that compiles down to a try/finally block and makes sure Dispose gets called even if an exception is thrown. Think of it as the C# way of guaranteeing cleanup happens the moment you're done, instead of sometime later.
keyPoints: You implement IDisposable when a type holds unmanaged resources like file handles, database connections, or COM objects, or when it subscribes to events that would otherwise keep it alive after you're finished with it.
The using statement compiles down to a try/finally block where Dispose gets called in the finally part, so cleanup is guaranteed to happen even if an exception is thrown inside the block.
The C# 8 using var declaration disposes the object at the end of the enclosing scope instead of needing a block, which cuts down on nesting and is now the preferred style.

The full dispose pattern includes a protected virtual Dispose(bool disposing) method, a public Dispose that calls it and then calls GC.SuppressFinalize to take the object off the finalizer queue, and a finalizer as a safety net for callers who forget to dispose.
For resources that need asynchronous cleanup, you implement IAsyncDisposable and use await using, which matters for things like flushing buffers or closing network connections asynchronously.

code.note: GC.SuppressFinalize removes the object from the finalization queue — avoids an extra GC cycle

redFlags: Not calling `GC.SuppressFinalize` in the full pattern.
Thinking `using` is only for DB connections.

dialogue.text: Explain `IDisposable` and `using`.
`Dispose()` deterministically frees unmanaged resources; `using` compiles to try/finally so `Dispose` runs even on exceptions.
When do you implement it?
When a type holds unmanaged resources or event subscriptions. The full pattern is `Dispose(bool)` plus `GC.SuppressFinalize(this)`, with a finalizer as a safety net.
What about async cleanup?
Implement `IAsyncDisposable` and use `await using` - for things like flushing a stream or closing a DB connection asynchronously.

followUpsQA.a: Dispose is deterministic; finalizer is a non-deterministic GC safety net.
To skip the finalizer and avoid an extra GC cycle.
C# 8 declaration that disposes at end of scope.
For async cleanup like flushing streams.

questions.q: What is the difference between managed and unmanaged code, and what is the CLR?

answer.oneLiner: Managed code runs under the supervision of the Common Language Runtime, which hands you garbage collection, JIT compilation, type safety, and exception handling automatically. Unmanaged code, like a native C or C++ DLL, runs straight on the CPU with no runtime safety net at all. When you write C# you're writing managed code, but you can still call into unmanaged code through P/Invoke or COM interop when you need something platform-specific.
keyPoints: The CLR is the runtime engine for .NET, and it provides JIT compilation, garbage collection, type safety, a structured exception model, and the security infrastructure for all of your managed code.
C# compiles to Intermediate Language, which is a platform-agnostic bytecode kind of like Java bytecode, and then the JIT compiler translates that IL into native machine code at runtime.
Modern .NET uses tiered compilation, where the code starts off with a quick tier-0 JIT and then gets recompiled with deeper optimisations in tier-1 once it's been spotted as a hot path.

You can call unmanaged code from C# using P/Invoke with the DllImport attribute, or through COM interop, but you're on the hook for marshalling the types correctly across the boundary between managed and unmanaged.

dialogue.text: Managed vs unmanaged code, and what's the CLR?
Managed code runs under the CLR with GC, type safety, and JIT; unmanaged code like C/C++ or COM runs natively without those services.
What does C# compile to?
IL - platform-agnostic bytecode - which the JIT compiles to native code at runtime, tiered in .NET Core.
How do you call unmanaged code?
Via P/Invoke with `DllImport`, or COM interop - and you manage the marshalling of types across the boundary.

followUpsQA.a: AOT compiles to native ahead of time; JIT at runtime.
Intermediate Language - the bytecode C# compiles to.
GC, JIT, type safety, exceptions, security.
With ildasm or a decompiler like ILSpy.

questions.q: Why is `string` immutable in C# and what are the implications?

answer.oneLiner: String is immutable in C# because once a string object is created its characters can never be changed, so any operation that looks like it's modifying a string is actually creating a brand new string object. That makes strings safe to share across threads without any synchronisation, but it also means concatenating strings in a loop is expensive because you're allocating a new object on every single iteration. For repeated concatenation you want StringBuilder, which keeps a single resizable internal buffer.
keyPoints: Because strings are immutable, every concatenation or manipulation creates a new string object rather than changing the existing one, which is exactly why sharing strings across threads is safe with no locking needed.
Concatenating strings in a loop with the plus operator is an O(n squared) allocation pattern, because each iteration builds a new string by copying all the previous characters plus the new ones.
StringBuilder keeps a single mutable char buffer that grows as it needs to, and then produces one final string when you call ToString, which makes repeated concatenation a lot more efficient.

The CLR interns string literals, so identical literal strings in your code share a single object in memory, which is also why reference equality comparisons between literals work correctly even though strings are reference types.

code.note: StringBuilder is backed by a resizable char array — one allocation at the end

dialogue.text: Why is `string` immutable?
Once created its contents never change, so every 'edit' makes a new object. That makes strings safe to share across threads and to use as keys.
What's the downside?
Concatenating in a loop allocates a new string each time - O(n^2) allocations. `StringBuilder` uses one growable buffer instead.
What is interning?
The CLR keeps a pool of string literals so identical literals share one instance, saving memory and enabling fast reference comparison.

followUpsQA.a: When concatenating in a loop or many unknown parts.
No mutation means no race on shared instances.

questions.q: What are nullable value types and nullable reference types in C#?

answer.oneLiner: Nullable value types let a normally non-nullable struct like an int hold a null, so when you write int question-mark it's really wrapping the value in Nullable of T and giving you a HasValue flag. Nullable reference types, added in C# 8, are a different thing entirely: they're just compile-time annotations that tell the compiler which references are allowed to be null, so it can warn you when you might be dereferencing a null. A good way to think about it is that one changes what the type can actually store at runtime, while the other is purely the compiler watching your back. I reach for nullable value types when a number is genuinely optional, and I turn on nullable reference types to catch null bugs before they ever ship.
keyPoints: Writing int question-mark is shorthand for Nullable of int, and you check it through HasValue and read it through Value.
You turn nullable reference types on with the Nullable enable setting in the project, and all it gives you is compiler warnings.
Nullable reference types don't change anything at runtime, they're purely a compile-time analysis.
The operators you lean on are the null-coalescing double question-mark, the null-conditional question-mark dot, and the null-forgiving bang, which you should use sparingly.

code.note: The null-forgiving `!` operator is a code smell — prefer proper null checks

dialogue.text: Nullable value types vs nullable reference types?
`int?` is `Nullable<int>`, a struct that can hold null at runtime. Nullable reference types (C# 8) are compile-time annotations that warn about possible null dereferences.
Does NRT change runtime behavior?
No - it's purely compiler flow analysis and warnings. The IL is unchanged; a `string?` is still just a reference at runtime.
How do you handle the warnings cleanly?
Use `?.`, `??`, and proper guards. The null-forgiving `!` suppresses a warning but is a smell - I use it sparingly.

followUpsQA.a: No - it just silences the compiler.

questions.q: What is `Dispose` vs `Finalize` (destructor)?

answer.oneLiner: Dispose is the cleanup you call yourself, usually through a using block, and it runs at a predictable moment right when you're done with the object, which is great for releasing things like file handles or database connections. A finalizer, the thing people sometimes call a destructor, is more of a safety net that the garbage collector runs at some unpredictable later time if you forgot to call Dispose. The two aren't really competitors: I always prefer Dispose for deterministic cleanup and treat the finalizer as a last resort. And inside Dispose I tell the garbage collector to skip the finalizer so the object doesn't pay that extra cost.
keyPoints: Finalizers run on a garbage collector thread at a time you can't predict or control.
An object that has a finalizer survives an extra garbage collection cycle, which makes it more expensive to collect.
Inside your Dispose method you should call GC.SuppressFinalize on this so the finalizer is skipped.
You should never touch other managed objects inside a finalizer, because they may already have been collected.

dialogue.text: Dispose vs Finalize?
`Dispose` is deterministic cleanup you call, usually via `using`. A finalizer is a non-deterministic GC safety net run on a finalizer thread.
Why prefer Dispose?
Finalized objects survive an extra GC cycle, which is expensive, and you can't control when the finalizer runs. So I call `GC.SuppressFinalize(this)` in `Dispose`.
Any rule about what a finalizer can touch?
Never touch other managed objects in a finalizer - they may already be collected. Only release unmanaged resources there.

followUpsQA.a: To skip the finalizer and avoid the extra GC cycle.
An extra GC generation/cycle for the object.

followUpsQA.q: Should every IDisposable have a finalizer?
followUpsQA.a: Only if it directly holds unmanaged resources.

questions.q: What is `Span<T>` and when should you use it?

answer.oneLiner: Span of T is a lightweight, stack-only view over a block of contiguous memory that lets you look at and slice that data without copying or allocating anything new. Think of it like a window onto an existing array or buffer: you can move the window around and read through it, but you're never duplicating the underlying bytes. It's a ref struct, which is what keeps it on the stack and makes it so fast and safe. I reach for it in hot paths like parsing or slicing big buffers where avoiding allocations really matters.
keyPoints: It's a ref struct, which means it lives only on the stack and can't be boxed or captured inside a lambda.
Calling Slice with a start and length hands you a window into the data without copying any of it.
When you only need to read, you use ReadOnlySpan of T, which is what you get from something like calling AsSpan on a string.
Memory of T is the heap-friendly, async-friendly cousin when you need something a Span can't do.

code.note: No substring allocation — Slice returns a view, not a copy

dialogue.text: What is `Span<T>` and when do you use it?
It's a stack-only `ref struct` giving a zero-allocation, type-safe view over contiguous memory - great for slicing arrays, strings, or stack buffers in hot paths.
What's the catch with it being a ref struct?
It can only live on the stack - you can't box it, store it in a field, capture it in a lambda, or use it across an `await`.
How do you parse without allocating substrings?
Use `AsSpan()` and `Slice` to get windows into the original buffer, then `int.Parse` on the span - no substring allocations at all.

followUpsQA.a: It's stack-only; the heap-based state machine can't hold it.

questions.q: What is the difference between `Span<T>` and `Memory<T>`?

answer.oneLiner: Span of T is a stack-only ref struct, so it's the fastest option with zero allocation, but the catch is it can't live on the heap, can't be a field, and can't survive across an await. Memory of T is the wrapper you use when you need exactly those things, because it can sit in a class field and be held across async calls. The way I usually work is to pass Memory around through my async code, then pull a Span out of it only at the moment I'm actually crunching the bytes. So the rule of thumb is Span for tight synchronous hot paths and Memory whenever async or storage gets involved.
keyPoints: Span of T is stack-only, so it can't be a class field, can't be captured, and can't be used across an await or a yield.
Memory of T is heap-friendly, so it works fine inside async methods and as a class field.
You get a Span out of a Memory through its Span property, and you only do that at the point where you actually process the buffer.
Both give you zero-copy views over data, so you pick Span for synchronous hot paths and Memory for anything async.

code.note: Rule: Memory<T> at the async boundary, Span<T> for the synchronous inner work

redFlags: Trying to store a Span<T> in a field or async local.
Not knowing why Span is stack-only (ref struct).

dialogue.text: Span vs Memory?
`Span<T>` is a stack-only ref struct - fastest, no allocation, but can't cross `await` or live on the heap. `Memory<T>` is heap-storable, so it works in async methods and fields.
So how do you combine them?
Use `Memory<T>` at the async boundary, then call `.Span` to do the synchronous processing - `await stream.ReadAsync(buffer)` then `Parse(buffer.Span)`.
Why exactly can't a Span cross an await?
An async method's locals become heap fields in the state machine, and a ref struct can't be a field - so the compiler forbids holding a Span across the suspension point.

followUpsQA.q: Can Span be a class field?

questions.q: Compare `string` concatenation, `StringBuilder`, and string interpolation — when do you use each?

answer.oneLiner: For a few known pieces of text, plain plus or string interpolation is perfectly fine and reads nicely. The moment you're building a string inside a loop or gluing together an unknown number of pieces, you want StringBuilder instead, because strings are immutable, so every plus creates a brand new string and copies everything over again. StringBuilder dodges that by keeping one growable buffer and only producing the final string once at the end. So my rule is interpolation for the simple cases and StringBuilder when the work happens in a loop.
keyPoints: Using plus or interpolation is fine for a fixed, small number of parts because it's basically one allocation.
Concatenating with plus inside a loop costs you order n-squared allocations, which is exactly when you switch to StringBuilder.
StringBuilder is a mutable buffer that grows as needed and only copies into a final string when you call ToString.
Interpolation with the dollar-sign string syntax reads cleanly, and since C# 10 it can also be very low overhead.

code.note: Threshold rule of thumb: if you concatenate in a loop, reach for StringBuilder

redFlags: Using StringBuilder for two or three fixed parts (premature optimization).
Building large strings with `+=` inside a loop.

dialogue.text: Concatenation vs StringBuilder vs interpolation - when each?
For a few known parts, `+` or interpolation is clearest and allocates once. For a loop or unknown number of pieces, `StringBuilder` - because strings are immutable and `+` reallocates each time.
Why is loop concatenation so bad?
Each `+=` builds a brand-new string by copying everything so far - that's O(n^2) total copying. `StringBuilder` keeps a growable buffer and copies once at `ToString()`.
Is StringBuilder always better then?
No - for two or three fixed parts it's overkill and less readable. Interpolation wins there, and since C# 10 it's also low-overhead.

followUpsQA.a: Each concat copies the whole accumulated string.
A small fixed number of parts.

questions.q: What is the difference between Server GC and Workstation GC, and when do you use each?

answer.oneLiner: Workstation garbage collection is tuned for low latency on a single heap and typically runs on the thread that triggered it, which suits desktop and client apps where you care about a responsive UI. Server garbage collection instead gives you a separate heap and a dedicated collection thread for each CPU core, which maximizes throughput when you've got lots of cores and lots of allocations, like a busy web server. The trade-off is that Server GC uses more memory in exchange for that throughput. So I leave Workstation on for client apps and switch to Server for high-load server workloads.
keyPoints: Workstation mode uses a single managed heap and collects on the calling thread, and it's the default for client apps.
Server mode gives each CPU its own heap and collection thread, so you get higher throughput at the cost of more memory.
Server GC is actually the default for ASP.NET Core and other hosted server workloads.
You turn it on by setting ServerGarbageCollection to true in the project file.

code.snippet:
<!-- .csproj -->
<PropertyGroup>
  <ServerGarbageCollection>true</ServerGarbageCollection>
  <ConcurrentGarbageCollection>true</ConcurrentGarbageCollection>
</PropertyGroup>
code.note: Server GC for throughput on many cores; Workstation for low-latency desktop/single-core

redFlags: Enabling Server GC on a memory-constrained single-core container blindly.
Not knowing ASP.NET Core defaults to Server GC.

dialogue.text: Server GC vs Workstation GC?
Workstation GC is tuned for low latency on a single heap and thread - good for desktop apps. Server GC uses a heap and dedicated GC thread per core for maximum throughput.
Which is the default where?
Workstation for client apps; Server GC is the default for ASP.NET Core and hosted server workloads. You enable it with `<ServerGarbageCollection>true</ServerGarbageCollection>`.
Any downside to Server GC?
It uses noticeably more memory - one heap per core - so on a memory-constrained single-core container it can hurt. I wouldn't enable it blindly there.

followUpsQA.a: Background collection of Gen 2 with less pause time.

followUpsQA.q: Why does Server GC use more memory?
followUpsQA.a: It maintains a separate heap per core.
Gen 0/1/2 with cheap frequent Gen 0 collections.

questions.q: How does `async`/`await` work in C#?

answer.oneLiner: When you mark a method async and use await, the compiler quietly rewrites it into a state machine, which is just a way of pausing the method at each await and picking up right where it left off once the awaited work finishes. The key thing is that while it's paused waiting on something like a network call, it isn't holding a thread hostage, the thread goes back to do other work. It's a bit like leaving a bookmark in a book so you can put it down and come back later without losing your place. That's why async is so good for I/O work where you'd otherwise be blocking a thread doing nothing.
keyPoints: Every await point becomes a state transition in a state machine the compiler generates for you.
While you're waiting on I/O, the thread is handed back to the pool so nothing is sitting there blocked.
When the work completes, the continuation resumes either on the captured context or on a thread pool thread.
You should return Task or ValueTask from async methods rather than void, with event handlers being the one exception.
answer.detail: Async I/O frees the thread while waiting, which is what makes servers scale — a blocking call holds the thread the whole time. `ValueTask<T>` avoids the `Task` allocation when a method usually completes synchronously; use only under measured allocation pressure.

code.note: The thread is free while waiting for the HTTP response — another request can be served

answer.analogy: Ordering food: you order and wait at your table (thread free); the waiter brings it when ready — you don't block the kitchen.
redFlags: Saying async/await creates a new thread.
Using `async void` outside event handlers.
Not knowing the compiler generates the state machine.

dialogue.text: How does async/await work in C#?
The compiler rewrites the `async` method into a state machine that suspends at each `await` and resumes when the awaited task completes - without blocking a thread.
So does `await` spin up a new thread?
No - for I/O the thread is released back to the pool while waiting; that's what lets servers scale. No thread is held during the wait.
Where does the continuation run?
On the captured `SynchronizationContext` if there is one (like a UI thread), otherwise on a thread-pool thread.

followUpsQA.a: Skips capturing the context so the continuation runs on the pool.
Hot paths that usually complete synchronously.
Its exceptions can crash the process and it can't be awaited.
No - I/O frees the thread instead.

questions.q: How can `.Result` or `.Wait()` cause a deadlock and how do you avoid it?

answer.oneLiner: A deadlock happens when you block on an async call with dot Result or dot Wait while you're sitting on a thread that has a synchronization context, like the UI thread. The problem is the awaited work wants to resume back on that exact same thread to finish, but that thread is frozen waiting for the result, so they're each waiting on the other forever. The clean fix is to just go async all the way and await instead of blocking. In library code you can also use ConfigureAwait false so the continuation doesn't insist on coming back to that captured thread.
keyPoints: The UI thread blocks on dot Result, but the continuation needs that same thread to run, so you get a circular wait.
ASP.NET Core has no synchronization context, so this deadlock is rarer there, though it's still bad practice.
The real fix is to go async all the way down and await instead of calling dot Result.
As a library workaround you can apply ConfigureAwait false so the continuation doesn't capture the context.

code.note: The only real fix is to not block on async code — go async end-to-end

redFlags: Not knowing WHY it deadlocks (SynchronizationContext).
Calling `ConfigureAwait(false)` the only fix.

dialogue.text: How can `.Result` or `.Wait()` cause a deadlock?

On a thread with a `SynchronizationContext` - like a UI or classic ASP.NET thread - blocking on `.Result` holds that thread, but the continuation needs the same thread to resume. Circular wait, deadlock.
How do you fix it?
Go async all the way - `await` instead of blocking. In library code, `ConfigureAwait(false)` avoids capturing the context so the continuation doesn't need the blocked thread.
Does ASP.NET Core still deadlock this way?
Rarely - ASP.NET Core removed the per-request SynchronizationContext, so there's no thread to contend for. Blocking is still bad practice though.

followUpsQA.a: The continuation needs the SyncContext thread that's blocked.
When you must block but want the unwrapped exception, not AggregateException.
It helps in libraries but going async is the real fix.

questions.q: What is the difference between `Task`, `Thread`, and `ThreadPool`?

answer.oneLiner: A Thread is a raw operating system thread, which is powerful but expensive to create and keep around. The ThreadPool is a set of reusable worker threads the runtime hands out so you're not constantly paying that creation cost. A Task is a higher-level idea, it represents some future piece of work, and for I/O work it isn't even necessarily tied to a thread at all while it waits. So I almost always work in terms of Tasks, fall back to the pool for short bits of CPU work, and only spin up a dedicated Thread for something genuinely long-running.
keyPoints: Creating a new Thread gives you a dedicated OS thread, which is what you want for long-running background work.
Task.Run is for handing short work items off to the thread pool.
For CPU-bound work you use Task.Run to offload it onto another thread.
For I/O-bound work you await the async call, and no thread is consumed while it's waiting.
answer.detail: Rule of thumb: CPU-bound → `Task.Run`; I/O-bound → `await` directly (no `Task.Run`); dedicated long-running → `new Thread` or `LongRunning`.

dialogue.text: Task vs Thread vs ThreadPool?
`Thread` is a raw, expensive OS thread; `ThreadPool` is reusable worker threads; `Task` is an abstraction over future work that, for I/O, isn't tied to a thread at all.
So how do you decide which to use?
CPU-bound short work - `Task.Run` onto the pool. I/O-bound - just `await` the async API, no thread consumed. Dedicated long-running work - a real `Thread` or `LongRunning`.
Why not Task.Run for I/O?
It just parks a pool thread waiting on I/O, wasting it. Async I/O frees the thread entirely while waiting.

followUpsQA.a: For long blocking work, so the pool isn't starved.

followUpsQA.q: Why does CPU vs I/O matter?
followUpsQA.a: CPU needs a thread; I/O can release it.

followUpsQA.q: Is a Task always a thread?
followUpsQA.a: No - I/O tasks use no thread while waiting.

questions.q: What is `CancellationToken` and when should you use it?

answer.oneLiner: A CancellationToken is how you do cooperative cancellation, meaning the caller can signal that it wants to stop, and the code doing the work checks for that signal and bows out cleanly. The caller raises the signal through a CancellationTokenSource, and the work being cancelled keeps an eye on the token and stops at a safe point. It's cooperative because nobody forcibly kills anything, the running code chooses when to honor the request. I pass the token down through all my async calls so cancellation flows all the way through, and in a web app it ties neatly into the request being aborted.
keyPoints: You create the token from a CancellationTokenSource and trigger it with Cancel or with CancelAfter for a timeout.
You should pass the token into every async call so the cancellation can flow all the way down.
Inside a tight CPU loop you check the token by calling ThrowIfCancellationRequested.
In ASP.NET Core, HttpContext.RequestAborted gives you a token that cancels when the client disconnects.

code.note: ASP.NET Core injects HttpContext.RequestAborted automatically for CancellationToken parameters

redFlags: Not threading the token through async calls.
Swallowing OperationCanceledException silently.

dialogue.text: What is a CancellationToken?
A cooperative cancellation signal. The caller cancels via a `CancellationTokenSource`, and the callee checks the token and stops cleanly.
How do you actually honor it?
Pass it into every async call, and in tight CPU loops call `token.ThrowIfCancellationRequested()`. It's cooperative - nothing force-kills your code.
Where does it come from in ASP.NET Core?
The framework injects `HttpContext.RequestAborted` for a `CancellationToken` parameter, so a client disconnect cancels the DB query downstream.

followUpsQA.a: `CancelAfter` combines both - a timeout is just a timed cancel.
No - let it propagate or handle it explicitly.

followUpsQA.q: How does a controller get one?
followUpsQA.a: Add a CancellationToken parameter - it's bound to RequestAborted.

questions.q: What is the difference between `async void` and `async Task`?

answer.oneLiner: The big difference is that async void can't be awaited, and if it throws, that exception has nowhere to go and can take down the whole process. With async Task the caller can await it and any exception flows back to them properly so they can handle it. Because of that, async Task is what you want basically everywhere, and the one place async void is acceptable is an event handler, because the event signature forces it. So my rule is async Task by default, async void only when an event handler leaves me no choice.
keyPoints: An exception thrown in an async void method goes unhandled up to the synchronization context and can crash the app.
Because async void can't be awaited, it's fire-and-forget and you have no control over when or whether it finished.
The only good reason to use async void is an event handler, where the signature is fixed for you.
If you genuinely need fire-and-forget, wrap the work in Task.Run with a try-catch around it.
redFlags: Using `async void` outside event handlers.
Not knowing its exceptions are unobserved.

dialogue.text: async void vs async Task?
`async Task` is awaitable and propagates exceptions; `async void` can't be awaited and its exceptions go unhandled to the context - which can crash the process.
So when is async void ever ok?
Only for event handlers, where the signature is fixed to `void`. Everywhere else use `async Task`.
How do you do fire-and-forget safely then?
Wrap the work in `Task.Run` with its own try/catch, or use a dedicated helper that observes and logs exceptions - never bare `async void`.

followUpsQA.q: Why are async void exceptions dangerous?
followUpsQA.a: They're unobserved and rethrown on the SyncContext.

followUpsQA.q: Only valid use of async void?

questions.q: What does `ConfigureAwait(false)` do and when should you use it?

answer.oneLiner: ConfigureAwait false is you telling await not to bother capturing the current synchronization context, so the continuation just resumes on any thread pool thread instead of marshaling back to a specific one. The main payoff is avoiding those classic blocking deadlocks in library code, since the continuation no longer insists on a thread that might be blocked. You mostly want it in reusable library code where you don't know or care what context the caller is on. The one place you avoid it is right before touching UI, because there you specifically need to be back on the UI thread.
keyPoints: By default, await captures the synchronization context and posts the continuation back to it.
ConfigureAwait false opts out of that, so the continuation just runs on whatever pool thread is free.
You use it in library code, whereas ASP.NET Core application code has no synchronization context to begin with.
You should not use it right before a UI update, because at that point you actually need to be on the UI thread.

code.note: Library code should always ConfigureAwait(false) to avoid deadlocking consumers

dialogue.text: What does ConfigureAwait(false) do?
It tells `await` not to capture the current `SynchronizationContext`, so the continuation runs on a thread-pool thread instead of marshaling back.
Where should you use it?
In library code, to avoid deadlocking consumers who block on your method. App code in ASP.NET Core has no SyncContext, so it doesn't matter there.
When must you NOT use it?
Before UI updates - the continuation has to run on the UI thread, so you keep the capture there.

followUpsQA.a: The continuation no longer needs the blocked context thread.
An abstraction controlling where continuations run.
No - you need the UI thread.

questions.q: What is `SynchronizationContext` and how does it affect `await`?

answer.oneLiner: A SynchronizationContext is the thing that decides how and where your continuations get scheduled after an await. In UI frameworks like WinForms or WPF it makes sure the continuation comes back to the UI thread, while ASP.NET Core simply doesn't have one. By default await grabs whatever context is current and resumes on it, which is exactly why UI code naturally lands back on the UI thread. Understanding this is what explains both why async just works in UI apps and why those blocking deadlocks happen, because no context means there's nothing to deadlock against.
keyPoints: In WinForms and WPF the synchronization context marshals continuations back onto the UI thread for you.
Classic ASP.NET had a per-request context so you could still reach HttpContext after an await.
ASP.NET Core removed the synchronization context, so all its threads are interchangeable.
That absence of a context is exactly why wrapping a call in Task.Run and then reading dot Result avoids the deadlock.

dialogue.text: What is a SynchronizationContext?
It controls how continuations are scheduled. UI frameworks post continuations back to the UI thread; ASP.NET Core has none, so threads are symmetric.
How does it affect await?
By default `await` captures the current context and resumes the continuation on it - that's why UI updates after `await` are safe in WPF/WinForms.
Why does removing it help ASP.NET Core?
No context means no thread contention, so blocking patterns that deadlock in classic ASP.NET don't deadlock the same way - and continuations are cheaper.

followUpsQA.q: How do you opt out of capture?
Why does UI code need it?
followUpsQA.a: To resume on the UI thread for control updates.

questions.q: What is the difference between parallelism and concurrency? How do `Task.WhenAll` and `Task.WhenAny` fit in?

answer.oneLiner: Concurrency is about multiple things making progress in an overlapping way, like one cook juggling several dishes by switching between them, while parallelism is literally doing things at the same instant on multiple cores, like several cooks each working their own dish. They're related but not the same, and you can have concurrency on a single thread just by interleaving awaits. Task.WhenAll is how you kick off a bunch of independent I/O operations concurrently and wait for them all, while something like Parallel.For is for spreading CPU-heavy work across cores. So I pick WhenAll for overlapping I/O and the parallel tools when I've got real CPU work to divide up.
keyPoints: Task.WhenAll runs a set of independent tasks concurrently and then waits for every one of them to finish.
Task.WhenAny completes as soon as the first task finishes, which makes it handy for timeouts.
For CPU-bound parallel work you use Parallel.For, Parallel.ForEach, or PLINQ.
Concurrency isn't the same as multi-threading, because awaits can interleave their progress on a single thread.

code.note: Start tasks before awaiting to run them concurrently

dialogue.text: Concurrency vs parallelism, and where do WhenAll/WhenAny fit?

Concurrency is overlapping progress - tasks interleave, even on one thread. Parallelism is literally running at once on multiple cores. `Task.WhenAll` enables concurrent I/O; `Parallel.For` enables CPU parallelism.
How do you make several I/O calls concurrent?
Start the tasks first, then `await Task.WhenAll` - three 1-second calls finish in about one second instead of three.
What's WhenAny good for?
Completing on the first task - useful for timeouts or racing a primary against a fallback source.

followUpsQA.a: WhenAll runs them concurrently; sequential awaits serialize.
Not necessarily - awaits can interleave on one thread.

questions.q: What actually happens under the hood when you `await` a Task? Explain the state machine.

answer.oneLiner: Under the hood the compiler takes your async method and generates a struct that implements IAsyncStateMachine, and the heart of it is a MoveNext method that's basically a switch over the different await points. Each time an awaited task completes, MoveNext gets called again and the switch jumps to the right spot to continue. Your local variables get promoted to fields on that struct so they survive across the awaits, since you've left and re-entered the method. And there's a nice optimization where if the task is already done, it just runs straight through synchronously instead of doing all the suspend-and-resume dance.
keyPoints: The body of your method gets turned into a switch statement driven by state numbers.
Your local variables are lifted into fields so their values survive across each await.
It plugs into the awaiter pattern, calling things like GetAwaiter and checking IsCompleted.
If an awaited task is already complete, it takes a fast path and runs synchronously.

dialogue.text: What happens under the hood when you await a Task?
The compiler generates a struct implementing `IAsyncStateMachine`. Its `MoveNext()` is a switch over await points, and it's called again when the awaited task completes.
How do locals survive across an await?
They're hoisted into fields of the state machine so they persist when the method suspends and resumes.
Is there a fast path?
Yes - if the awaited task is already complete, it runs synchronously without yielding, avoiding the suspend/resume overhead.

followUpsQA.q: What interface does the state machine implement?

followUpsQA.a: Via IValueTaskSource and the struct itself on the sync path.
A state machine plus possible Task allocation.

questions.q: What is the difference between `ValueTask` and `Task`, and when does `ValueTask` help?

answer.oneLiner: Task is a class, so it's a reference type that always gets allocated on the heap, whereas ValueTask is a struct that can skip that allocation when a method frequently finishes synchronously and already has the answer ready. So ValueTask shines on really hot, frequently-called paths where you'd otherwise be churning out a lot of Task allocations for results that were available immediately. The catch is it comes with rules, like you can only await it once and you shouldn't block on it. My default is plain Task, and I only switch to ValueTask once I've actually measured that allocations are a problem.
keyPoints: Task always allocates on the heap, while ValueTask can wrap an immediate result with no allocation at all.
You reach for ValueTask on hot paths that usually return a cached or synchronously-available result.
It comes with restrictions, namely you await it only once, never block on it, and don't store it away to await again.
Default to Task and only move to ValueTask once you've measured real allocation pressure.

code.note: ValueTask shines when the synchronous (cache-hit) path dominates

redFlags: Using ValueTask everywhere by default.
Awaiting the same ValueTask more than once or calling .Result on it.

dialogue.text: ValueTask vs Task?
`Task` is a heap-allocated reference type; `ValueTask` is a struct that avoids the allocation when the method often completes synchronously - like a cache hit.
When does ValueTask actually help?
On hot, frequently-called paths that usually return a cached or synchronous result, so you skip the per-call Task allocation.
What are the gotchas?
You must await it only once, never block on it, and never store and await it twice - otherwise behavior is undefined. So I default to Task and switch only under measured pressure.

followUpsQA.q: Why not await a ValueTask twice?
followUpsQA.a: Its backing source may be reused/pooled - undefined behavior.
Task - switch to ValueTask only when measured.
An interface enabling pooled, zero-alloc ValueTasks.

questions.q: What does the `volatile` keyword do and when is it needed?

answer.oneLiner: Marking a field volatile forces every read and write to go straight to main memory with what's called acquire and release semantics, which stops both the compiler and the CPU from caching the value or reordering the operations around it. The practical need for this is a simple flag shared between threads, where one thread sets it and another needs to actually see the change rather than a stale cached copy. It's a fairly low-level tool and only really suits those simple single-variable flags. For anything more, like incrementing a counter, volatile isn't enough and you reach for stronger tools.
keyPoints: It stops a thread from holding onto a stale copy of the field cached in a register.
It also prevents certain instruction reorderings from happening around that access.
It only suits simple flags, and importantly it does not make a compound operation like a plus-plus increment atomic.
For counters you use Interlocked, and for protecting blocks of code you use lock, since both give you stronger guarantees.

code.note: volatile is for a single shared flag — not a substitute for lock/Interlocked

redFlags: Thinking volatile makes compound operations atomic.
Using volatile when a lock or Interlocked is actually required.

dialogue.text: What does `volatile` do?
It forces reads/writes of a field to hit main memory with acquire/release semantics, stopping the compiler/CPU from caching the value in a register or reordering accesses.
When do you actually need it?
For a simple lock-free flag shared across threads - like a `bool _stop` set by one thread and polled by another.
Does it make `count++` thread-safe?
No - `++` is read-modify-write, three steps. `volatile` only orders individual reads and writes. For counters use `Interlocked`.

followUpsQA.q: Why isn't count++ atomic with volatile?
followUpsQA.a: It's a read-modify-write, not a single operation.
volatile = ordering only; Interlocked = atomic ops; lock = full mutual exclusion.

questions.q: Compare `lock`, `Monitor`, `Mutex`, and `Semaphore`/`SemaphoreSlim` for synchronization.

answer.oneLiner: The lock keyword is really just convenient syntax over Monitor, and it's a fast in-process lock that lets exactly one thread into a section at a time. A Mutex is a heavier, operating-system-level lock whose advantage is that it can coordinate across separate processes, not just threads in one app. Semaphore and SemaphoreSlim are different in that they let a set number of holders in at once rather than just one, so they act like a throttle, and SemaphoreSlim is the one that supports async. So I default to lock for simple mutual exclusion and pick the others when I need cross-process coordination, limited concurrency, or async waiting.
keyPoints: lock and Monitor give you fast in-process mutual exclusion where only one thread is in the section at a time.
A Mutex is a named, cross-process kernel object that's slower, useful for things like enforcing a single running instance of an app.
Semaphore and SemaphoreSlim limit access to a set number of concurrent threads, acting like a throttle.
SemaphoreSlim's WaitAsync is the only one of these you can actually await.

code.note: Never await inside a lock; use SemaphoreSlim for async mutual exclusion

redFlags: Trying to `await` inside a `lock`.
Using a cross-process Mutex for simple in-process locking.

dialogue.text: Compare lock, Monitor, Mutex, and Semaphore.

`lock` is sugar over `Monitor` - fast, in-process, one thread. `Mutex` is a heavier OS-level lock that works across processes. `Semaphore`/`SemaphoreSlim` allow N concurrent holders, and `SemaphoreSlim` supports async.
When is a Mutex worth its cost?
Only when you need cross-process coordination - like enforcing a single instance of an app. For in-process locking it's overkill.
How do you do mutual exclusion around an await?
You can't `lock` across `await`. Use `SemaphoreSlim.WaitAsync()` with a try/finally `Release()` - it's the only awaitable one.

followUpsQA.q: Why can't you await inside lock?
followUpsQA.a: Monitor is thread-affine; the continuation may resume on another thread.

questions.q: How do you implement a producer-consumer pattern in modern C#?

answer.oneLiner: In modern C# I'd reach for System.Threading.Channels to build an async producer-consumer setup, or BlockingCollection of T if I'm working in the older blocking, thread-based style.
