Both give you a thread-safe queue sitting between the side that produces items and the side that consumes them, along with backpressure so things don't run away. The idea is a producer keeps writing work into the queue while one or more consumers pull items off and process them, all safely coordinated. Channels are my go-to today because they're async-first, while BlockingCollection fits classic worker-thread code.
keyPoints: Channel of T is the async-first choice, with WriteAsync and ReadAllAsync, and it supports bounded backpressure.
BlockingCollection of T offers a blocking API that suits classic dedicated worker threads.
The producer signals it's finished by calling Complete on the writer, or CompleteAdding on a BlockingCollection.
A bounded channel applies backpressure so that a fast producer can't outrun the consumer and exhaust memory.

code.note: Channels are the modern async-friendly choice; BlockingCollection for blocking threads

redFlags: Hand-rolling a lock + Queue when Channels exist.
Forgetting to Complete the writer (consumers hang forever).

dialogue.text: How do you implement producer-consumer in modern C#?
Use `System.Threading.Channels` for async producer-consumer, or `BlockingCollection<T>` for the older blocking-thread style. Both give a thread-safe queue with backpressure.
How does backpressure work?
A bounded channel blocks (asynchronously) the producer when full, so a fast producer can't exhaust memory. The producer calls `Writer.Complete()` when done.
How do you run multiple consumers?
Spin up several tasks all doing `await foreach (var item in reader.ReadAllAsync())` - the channel hands each item to one consumer safely.

followUpsQA.q: What does a full bounded channel do?
followUpsQA.a: Awaits the producer until space frees up.
Channels are safer and built for this.

questions.q: What is the difference between `Parallel.ForEach` and PLINQ (`AsParallel`)?

answer.oneLiner: Both of these let you spread CPU-heavy work across multiple cores so it finishes faster. Parallel.ForEach is the imperative one, you use it when you just want to run some action on each item in a collection, like resizing a thousand images. PLINQ, which you turn on by adding AsParallel to a LINQ query, is the declarative one where you describe a query pipeline that produces a result and it gives you clean ways to control ordering. Neither one is meant for waiting on network or disk; for that you'd use Task.WhenAll instead.
keyPoints: Parallel.ForEach is the imperative choice and it's great when you just want to do some action for each item.
PLINQ, which you switch on with AsParallel, is declarative LINQ that returns a transformed sequence as its result.
PLINQ gives you clean helpers like AsOrdered to keep order and WithDegreeOfParallelism to cap how many threads it uses.
Neither of these is for I/O-bound work like network or disk calls; for that you'd reach for Task.WhenAll instead.

code.note: Parallel.ForEach for side effects; PLINQ to compute a result set

redFlags: Using either for I/O-bound work instead of async.
Mutating shared state inside the body without synchronization.

dialogue.text: Parallel.ForEach vs PLINQ?
Both parallelize CPU-bound work across cores. `Parallel.ForEach` is for imperative side-effecting loops; PLINQ (`AsParallel`) is for declarative query pipelines that return a result.
When does parallelizing actually hurt?
When the per-item work is tiny - the partitioning and synchronization overhead outweighs the gain, and it can be slower than a plain loop.
What about I/O-bound work?
Neither - those block threads. For I/O use `Task.WhenAll` with async calls instead.

questions.q: What is a race condition, and how do you detect and prevent one?

answer.oneLiner: A race condition is when the outcome of your code depends on the exact timing of two or more threads touching the same shared data at the same time, so it works fine most of the time but occasionally gives a wrong answer. A classic example is two threads both reading a counter, both adding one, and both writing back, so you lose an increment. You prevent it either by not sharing mutable data at all, or by guarding the shared data with something like a lock or the Interlocked helpers.
keyPoints: The classic cause is a read-modify-write like count plus plus, where two threads interleave and one update gets lost.
You prevent it with a lock, with Interlocked for simple counters, or by using immutable or per-thread data so nothing is shared.
It's often cleaner to use a thread-safe type like ConcurrentDictionary instead of locking everything by hand.
You detect race conditions through stress and load testing, careful code review of any shared fields, and tools like race detectors or logging.

code.note: Interlocked is the cheapest fix for simple counters; lock for multi-step updates

redFlags: Assuming `count++` is atomic.
Adding locks everywhere without identifying the shared state.

dialogue.text: What is a race condition?
When the result depends on the unpredictable timing of concurrent threads touching shared mutable state - like two threads doing `count++` and losing updates.
How do you prevent it?
Remove shared mutability, or guard it - `Interlocked` for counters, `lock` for multi-step updates, or thread-safe types like `ConcurrentDictionary`.
How do you detect one?
Stress and load tests, careful review of shared fields, and logging - they're timing-dependent so they hide in normal testing.

followUpsQA.a: Race is bad timing on shared state; deadlock is mutual blocking.
No - the factory can run more than once.

questions.q: What is `IAsyncEnumerable<T>` (async streams) and when do you use it?

answer.oneLiner: IAsyncEnumerable, which arrived in C# 8, is a way to stream a sequence of items asynchronously, so you can pull them in one at a time as they become available instead of waiting for the whole set. You consume it with await foreach, and you produce it with an async method that uses yield return. It's perfect for things like reading a paged API or streaming rows from a database, because you never have to buffer everything in memory at once.
keyPoints: You consume it with await foreach, and you produce it from an async method that returns IAsyncEnumerable and uses yield return.
It's both lazy and async, so each item awaits its I/O and you never load the whole set into memory.
You make it cancellable by accepting a CancellationToken marked with the EnumeratorCancellation attribute.
It's ideal for paging through APIs, streaming query results, or reading large files and streams piece by piece.

code.note: Streams pages without ever holding all results in memory

redFlags: Materializing the whole stream with ToList when streaming was the point.
Not flowing the CancellationToken into the iterator.

dialogue.text: What is IAsyncEnumerable and when do you use it?
It's an async stream (C# 8) - you `await foreach` over it, and produce items with `async` plus `yield return`. Great for paged APIs or streaming DB reads without buffering everything.
Why not just return a List?
A list materializes everything in memory. `IAsyncEnumerable` is lazy and async - each item awaits I/O as it's pulled, so you process huge sets with a small footprint.
How do you make it cancellable?
Take a `[EnumeratorCancellation] CancellationToken` parameter so the token flows into the iterator and the consumer's token reaches your awaits.

followUpsQA.a: It wires the consumer's token into the generated iterator.
Streams rows vs buffering them all.

questions.q: Walk me through exactly what happens the moment execution hits an `await` on an in-flight I/O call.

answer.oneLiner: When you hit an await, the method actually runs normally up to that point. If the task you're awaiting isn't finished yet, the method hands its own Task back to whoever called it and the current thread is freed up to go do other work instead of sitting there blocked. Later, when the I/O finishes, a continuation kicks in and resumes the rest of your method right where it left off.
keyPoints: Behind the scenes the compiler rewrites your method into a state machine that remembers your local variables and where to pick back up.
If the task you await is already done, the method just keeps running straight through with no thread switch at all.
If the task isn't done yet, the thread is handed back to the pool rather than sitting there blocked and waiting.
When the work completes, the continuation runs, restores your saved state, and carries on from right after the await.

code.note: No thread sits and waits during the network call — that is the whole point.

dialogue.text: Say I have a handler that calls an external payment API with await. The instant we hit that await, what is the thread doing?

It runs synchronously right up to the await. If the API call hasn't completed yet, the method returns its own Task to the caller and the thread goes back to the pool to serve other work - it isn't sitting and waiting.
So how does the rest of my method ever run after the response comes back?

The compiler turned the method into a state machine that saved my locals and a resume point. When the payment task completes it schedules a continuation that restores that state and runs everything after the await.
What if the payment result was already cached and the task is already complete at the await?
Then there's no thread hop at all - await sees a completed task and just keeps running synchronously on the same thread, which is a nice fast path.

followUpsQA.a: No - completed tasks continue synchronously; only pending ones schedule a continuation.

followUpsQA.q: Where are my local variables stored across the await?
followUpsQA.a: In the generated state-machine struct/object, not on the original stack.
A continuation scheduled on the captured context or the thread pool.

followUpsQA.q: Is the calling thread blocked during the await?
followUpsQA.a: No - it is released; that is how async scales.

questions.q: An endpoint calls `service.GetAsync().Result` instead of awaiting. Under load it hangs and throughput collapses — explain why and fix it.

answer.oneLiner: When you call .Result or .Wait() you block a pool thread that just sits there doing nothing while it waits for the I/O to come back. Under heavy load every incoming request grabs and holds a thread that way, the thread pool can't add new threads fast enough to keep up, and you end up with thread-pool starvation where requests pile up and throughput falls off a cliff. It can even deadlock outright. The fix is to await the whole way down instead of blocking.
keyPoints: Blocking holds a thread hostage for the entire length of the I/O call instead of letting it go do other work.
The pool only grows by about one thread every 500 milliseconds, so a sudden burst drains it, requests start queuing, and latency spikes.
Doing sync over async can also flat-out deadlock anywhere a SynchronizationContext is present.
The real fix is to make the whole call chain async from top to bottom and never block on an async call.

code.note: Each blocked thread can't serve other requests; awaiting frees it for the pool.

dialogue.text: We have an action that does service.GetAsync().Result. It's fine in dev but in prod under traffic the whole service freezes. What's going on?
Each request grabs a thread-pool thread and then blocks it on .Result for the entire downstream I/O. Under load every concurrent request holds a thread doing nothing, so the pool runs dry.
But the pool can grow new threads, right? Why doesn't it just recover?
It injects new threads slowly - roughly one every half second. A traffic burst exhausts threads far faster than that, so requests queue up and latency explodes; that's thread-pool starvation.
What's the fix, and is there any case where blocking is acceptable?
Await the whole chain so the thread is released during I/O. Blocking is only OK at a true top-level entry point that can't be async, like a console Main before C# 7.1 - never inside request handling.

followUpsQA.q: Why does awaiting scale better than blocking?
followUpsQA.a: Awaiting releases the thread during I/O so one thread can serve many requests.

followUpsQA.q: How fast does the thread pool add threads?
followUpsQA.a: Roughly one new thread per 500ms by default - too slow for bursts.
Yes, in contexts with a SynchronizationContext like UI or classic ASP.NET.

followUpsQA.q: Quick way to spot the problem?
followUpsQA.a: Rising thread count plus queued requests and CPU mostly idle.

questions.q: A teammate wrote `public async void SaveAsync()` for a service method. What's the risk, and when is `async void` ever the right choice?

answer.oneLiner: The problem with async void is that nobody can await it, so the caller has no way to know when it finished or whether it failed, and any exception it throws escapes onto the SynchronizationContext where it can crash the whole process instead of being captured safely in a Task. So for a normal method you should return async Task instead. The one and only legitimate place for async void is an event handler, because their signature has to be void.
keyPoints: Because the caller can't await it, it becomes fire-and-forget with no way to know it finished and no way to catch its errors.
Any unhandled exception gets thrown onto the context or thread pool and can take down the entire app.
If you return a Task or Task of T instead, callers can await it and actually observe both the result and any errors.
The only place async void is acceptable is an event handler, since those are required to have a void return type.

code.note: Only event handlers justify async void; everything else returns Task.

dialogue.text: Code review: someone made a repository helper async void. Why would I push back on that?

Because callers can't await it - they can't tell when the save finished or whether it failed. And if SaveAsync throws, the exception escapes onto the context instead of into a Task, which can crash the whole process.
Where exactly does that exception go that makes it so dangerous?
There's no Task to put it on, so it's re-raised on the captured SynchronizationContext or the thread pool as an unhandled exception - no try/catch around the call can catch it.
So is async void ever correct?
Yes, for event handlers - their signature has to return void, like a button Click handler. Even then I keep the body tiny and delegate to an awaitable Task method so I can test and handle errors.

followUpsQA.q: Why can't you await async void?
followUpsQA.a: It returns void, so there's no Task to await or observe.

followUpsQA.q: Where do async void exceptions land?
followUpsQA.a: On the SynchronizationContext or thread pool as unhandled - they can crash the app.
Event handlers, whose delegate signature must return void.

followUpsQA.q: Async void vs async Task difference?
followUpsQA.a: Task is awaitable and captures exceptions; void is fire-and-forget.

questions.q: A dashboard needs data from 3 independent microservices, each ~200ms. It currently awaits them one after another. How do you make it fast?

answer.oneLiner: Right now the code awaits each service one after another, so they run back to back and the times stack up to around 600 milliseconds. The trick is to kick off all three calls first without awaiting, then do await Task.WhenAll on them, which lets all three run at the same time. Since they're independent and waiting on the network, the total time drops to roughly the slowest single call, about 200 milliseconds.
keyPoints: Awaiting one call before starting the next forces independent I/O to run in sequence, so the durations add up.
Instead you start all the tasks first and then await Task.WhenAll, which lets them overlap and run together.
Because the calls are independent and I/O-bound, the total time becomes roughly the slowest call rather than the sum of all of them.
WhenAll surfaces the first exception to you and quietly keeps the rest stored on the task it returns.

code.note: Start the tasks before awaiting; .Result is safe only after WhenAll has completed them.

dialogue.text: My dashboard calls three independent services sequentially and feels sluggish - each is about 200ms. How would you speed it up?

Right now you're paying 600ms because each await blocks the next call from even starting. Since they're independent, I'd kick off all three tasks first and then await Task.WhenAll, so they overlap and you're down to about 200ms.
Why does just moving the awaits make them run concurrently?
Calling GetAsync starts the I/O immediately and returns a hot task; if I don't await right away, all three are already in flight. WhenAll just waits for the set to finish.
If two of those calls throw, what does WhenAll give you?
Awaiting WhenAll rethrows the first exception, but the returned task holds all of them in its Exception property, so I can inspect the full AggregateException if I need every failure.

followUpsQA.q: Why is sequential slower for independent calls?
followUpsQA.a: Each await waits for the prior call before starting the next, so times add up.
Not necessarily - I/O completions overlap without dedicating threads.

followUpsQA.q: How does WhenAll report multiple errors?
followUpsQA.a: Await rethrows one; the task's Exception holds all as an AggregateException.

followUpsQA.q: When would you still await sequentially?
followUpsQA.a: When a later call depends on an earlier call's result.

questions.q: You call a flaky external API and want to give up if it doesn't answer within 2 seconds. How do you implement first-to-respond / timeout with tasks?

answer.oneLiner: You race the actual call against a timer using Task.WhenAny, and whichever one finishes first is the winner. If the timer wins, that's your timeout, so you cancel the real call and throw. In newer .NET there's an even cleaner built-in way to do exactly this with task.WaitAsync and a timeout.
keyPoints: Task.WhenAny hands you back the first task that finishes, so you can pit your real work against a timer.
You pair it with a CancellationTokenSource so that when the timer wins, the losing call actually gets cancelled.
You should still observe the loser afterward so its result or exception doesn't go unobserved.
On .NET 6 and later you can just await task.WaitAsync with a TimeSpan and a token as a built-in shortcut.

code.note: WhenAny picks the winner; the CTS makes sure the loser is cancelled, not orphaned.

dialogue.text: We integrate with a partner API that sometimes stalls. I want a hard 2-second budget. How would you build that?
I'd race the API call against a Task.Delay of 2 seconds with Task.WhenAny. If the delay finishes first, that's my timeout signal and I throw; otherwise I return the API result.
When the timeout fires, the API call is still running in the background. Doesn't that leak?

Yes, which is why I pass a CancellationToken into the call and cancel the CTS on timeout, so the underlying request actually aborts instead of dangling. I also observe the losing task to avoid an unobserved exception.
Is there a simpler built-in way now?
On .NET 6 and later I'd just use task.WaitAsync with a timeout - it handles the race and throws TimeoutException for me, so I only write the WhenAny version when I need custom behavior.

followUpsQA.a: The first task to complete; you inspect which one to act on it.
So the losing call stops consuming resources instead of running orphaned.
An unawaited faulted task can raise an unobserved-exception event.

questions.q: Where do exceptions thrown inside an async method actually surface, and how does that change when you don't await?

answer.oneLiner: When an async method throws, the exception gets captured and stored on the Task it returns, and then it gets re-thrown at the moment you await that task. That means a plain old try/catch around the await works just like it would for normal synchronous code. But if you never await the task, or you block on it with .Result, the exception stays wrapped inside an AggregateException or quietly goes unobserved.
keyPoints: The exception is parked on the Task and then thrown again at the point where you await it.
A try/catch wrapped around the await catches it exactly like it would catch a synchronous exception.
If you use .Result or .Wait() instead, you get an AggregateException that wraps the real exception inside.
A faulted task that nobody ever awaits ends up raising the TaskScheduler.UnobservedTaskException event.

code.note: await gives you the original exception; .Wait()/.Result wrap it in AggregateException.

dialogue.text: If an async method throws, where do I catch it? Does a regular try/catch even work?
Yes - the exception is captured on the returned Task and rethrown when you await, so a normal try/catch around the await catches the original exception type just like sync code.
We had a service where the exception showed up as AggregateException and our catch missed it. Why?

That happens when you block with .Result or .Wait instead of awaiting - those wrap everything in an AggregateException. The fix is to await, which unwraps to the real exception, or inspect InnerException if you must block.
What if a task throws but nobody ever awaits it?
It becomes an unobserved task exception - the failure is silent until the task is collected, when TaskScheduler.UnobservedTaskException fires. That's why fire-and-forget needs an explicit catch.

followUpsQA.q: Why does await unwrap but .Result wrap?
followUpsQA.a: Await rethrows the first inner exception; .Result throws the AggregateException directly.
Yes - it behaves like synchronous exception handling.
Await throws the first; the task holds all in AggregateException.
Wrap the body in try/catch or attach a continuation that logs faults.

questions.q: A user starts a report that takes 30s, then closes the browser. How do you cancel the work end-to-end with `CancellationToken`?

answer.oneLiner: Cancellation in .NET is cooperative, which means nothing gets forcibly killed; instead you pass a single CancellationToken from the very top all the way down through every async call. In ASP.NET Core you can hook into HttpContext.RequestAborted, which fires when the user disconnects. Each layer just forwards the same token down, and the lowest-level I/O calls or your own loops check it and throw an OperationCanceledException when cancellation is requested.
keyPoints: It's cooperative, so nothing gets force-killed; your code has to actually check the token and respond to it.
You thread the very same token through every layer, right down to the actual I/O call at the bottom.
ASP.NET Core hands you HttpContext.RequestAborted, or you can just accept a CancellationToken as an action parameter.
Inside long-running loops between I/O steps you call token.ThrowIfCancellationRequested to bail out promptly.

code.note: Same token, top to bottom; cancellation surfaces as OperationCanceledException.

dialogue.text: A 30-second report is running and the client disconnects. I want the server to stop doing the work. How do you wire that up?

Cancellation is cooperative, so I accept a CancellationToken on the action - in ASP.NET Core it's automatically bound to RequestAborted - and pass that same token all the way down to the database call and any loops.
What does passing it down actually accomplish?

The lowest-level async I/O honors the token and throws OperationCanceledException when it's signaled, which unwinds the whole call chain. For CPU loops I add ThrowIfCancellationRequested between iterations so it stops promptly.
Should I catch that OperationCanceledException and turn it into a 500?
No - a cancellation isn't an error. I let it propagate; ASP.NET Core treats a request-aborted cancellation as expected and won't log it as a failure.

followUpsQA.a: No - it's cooperative; code must check the token to stop.

followUpsQA.q: Where does ASP.NET Core get the token?
followUpsQA.a: From HttpContext.RequestAborted, bound to a CancellationToken parameter.

followUpsQA.q: Should you treat OperationCanceledException as an error?
followUpsQA.a: No - it's expected; let it propagate rather than logging a failure.

questions.q: Should you put `ConfigureAwait(false)` in your ASP.NET Core controllers? What about in a NuGet library you ship?

answer.oneLiner: ASP.NET Core doesn't have a SynchronizationContext, so adding ConfigureAwait(false) there literally does nothing, and you can just leave it out of your app code to keep things clean. But in a reusable library that you ship, you have no idea what kind of caller will use it, so you put ConfigureAwait(false) on every await to avoid forcing your continuations back onto the caller's context, which might be a blocking single-threaded one.
keyPoints: ConfigureAwait(false) is basically saying don't bother resuming on the captured context, just use the thread pool.
Since ASP.NET Core has no SynchronizationContext, it has no effect there, so there's no point cluttering your controllers with it.
In library code you add it on every await to stay context-agnostic and avoid causing deadlocks for whoever calls you.
UI apps like WPF and WinForms need the default of true so the continuation comes back to the UI thread.

code.note: Default in libraries: ConfigureAwait(false). In ASP.NET Core app code: don't bother.

dialogue.text: Our static analyzer flags every await in our ASP.NET Core controllers for missing ConfigureAwait(false). Should we add it?
No - ASP.NET Core doesn't have a SynchronizationContext, so ConfigureAwait(false) is a no-op there. Adding it everywhere just adds noise without changing behavior.
So when does it actually matter?

In reusable libraries. You don't know who calls you - a WPF app or legacy ASP.NET might block on your task with a SyncContext. Using ConfigureAwait(false) keeps continuations off that context and avoids deadlocking your callers.
Any place where ConfigureAwait(false) is actually wrong?
In UI code that touches controls after the await - you need the default so the continuation resumes on the UI thread. ConfigureAwait(false) there would throw a cross-thread access exception.

followUpsQA.a: Resumes the continuation on the thread pool instead of the captured context.

followUpsQA.q: Why no effect in ASP.NET Core?
followUpsQA.a: It has no SynchronizationContext to capture.
To stay context-agnostic and avoid deadlocking blocking callers.
In UI continuations that must run on the UI thread.

questions.q: A WPF button handler calls a legacy library method that does `SomeAsync().Result`, and the UI freezes forever. Diagnose and fix the deadlock.

answer.oneLiner: What's happening is the UI thread calls .Result and blocks while still holding the single-threaded UI SynchronizationContext. Meanwhile the awaited continuation needs that exact same UI thread to resume on, but that thread is stuck blocked waiting, so neither side can move. It's the classic sync-over-async deadlock. You fix it by awaiting the whole way through, or by having the library use ConfigureAwait(false) so its continuation doesn't need the UI thread.
keyPoints: The UI has a one-thread SynchronizationContext, so by default the continuation gets queued right back onto that thread.
Calling .Result blocks that thread, which means the continuation can never run, and the two sides deadlock each other.
The real fix is to make the handler async and await the call instead of blocking on it.
As a library fix or mitigation, ConfigureAwait(false) lets the continuation skip the UI context entirely.

code.note: Never block on async from a thread that owns a single-threaded context.

dialogue.text: A WPF app freezes the moment a button is clicked. The handler calls a library method that does SomeAsync().Result. Walk me through why it hangs.

The click runs on the UI thread, which has a single-threaded SynchronizationContext. .Result blocks that thread. But the await inside the library captured that context and wants to resume its continuation on the UI thread - which is blocked - so neither side can proceed.
Why does the continuation insist on the UI thread in the first place?

Because by default await captures the current SynchronizationContext and posts the continuation back to it. In a UI app that's the UI thread, so the blocked thread is exactly the one the continuation is waiting for.
Give me the proper fix versus a quick mitigation.

The proper fix is async all the way - make the handler async and await instead of blocking, so the thread is free to run the continuation. A quick mitigation is having the library await with ConfigureAwait(false) so the continuation doesn't need the UI context.

followUpsQA.q: What two things must coincide for this deadlock?
followUpsQA.a: A single-threaded SyncContext plus blocking on the async call.

followUpsQA.q: Why doesn't this deadlock in ASP.NET Core?
followUpsQA.a: It has no SynchronizationContext, so the continuation runs on the pool.
Await all the way down instead of blocking.

questions.q: You must export a 5-million-row query to CSV without blowing up memory. How do you stream it with `IAsyncEnumerable` / `await foreach`?

answer.oneLiner: You return an IAsyncEnumerable and await foreach over it, so the rows flow through one batch at a time instead of being pulled into one enormous list that blows up memory. EF Core's AsAsyncEnumerable streams the rows straight from the database, and as each row arrives you write it directly to the output file. That keeps your memory usage flat no matter how many rows there are.
keyPoints: Using await foreach pulls items lazily and asynchronously, so memory stays constant instead of growing with the row count.
In EF Core, AsAsyncEnumerable streams the results, whereas ToListAsync buffers the entire result set in memory.
You write each row out to the response or file the moment it arrives rather than collecting them all first.
You flow a CancellationToken marked with EnumeratorCancellation through it so that a disconnect stops the stream.

code.note: ToListAsync would load all 5M rows into RAM; AsAsyncEnumerable streams them.

dialogue.text: We need a CSV export of about 5 million rows and the current ToListAsync version OOMs. How do you fix it?

ToListAsync buffers every row in memory first. I'd switch to streaming - expose an IAsyncEnumerable and use EF's AsAsyncEnumerable, then await foreach and write each row straight to the CSV stream so memory stays flat.
How is await foreach different from just looping a normal collection?
Each iteration awaits the next item as it's fetched, so rows are pulled lazily from the DB instead of all at once. Combined with writing as we go, we never hold more than a small buffer in memory.
If the client cancels the download halfway, does the DB query keep running?
Not if I flow the token - I add an [EnumeratorCancellation] CancellationToken and pass it via WithCancellation, so cancelling breaks out of the await foreach and stops pulling from the database.

followUpsQA.a: It materializes the entire result set before returning.

followUpsQA.q: How does await foreach keep memory flat?
followUpsQA.a: It pulls and processes one item at a time, lazily.
AsAsyncEnumerable() streams rows from the database.

questions.q: You need to call an API for each of 500 order IDs. Compare `await`-in-a-`foreach` vs collecting tasks and `WhenAll` — and what could go wrong with the parallel version?

answer.oneLiner: If you await inside the foreach, the 500 calls run strictly one at a time, which is slow but gentle on the API. If you instead collect all the tasks and then await Task.WhenAll, they all fire concurrently and it's much faster, but firing 500 calls at once can hammer the API and exhaust your connections. So the right answer is the concurrent approach but throttled, using a SemaphoreSlim to cap how many run at once, or Parallel.ForEachAsync with a MaxDegreeOfParallelism limit.
keyPoints: Awaiting inside the loop runs everything sequentially, so the total time is the sum of all 500 calls.
Building up a list of tasks and then awaiting Task.WhenAll runs them concurrently, so the total is roughly the slowest single call.
Letting all 500 run unbounded risks hitting rate limits, exhausting sockets and connections, and overwhelming the downstream service.
You throttle it with a SemaphoreSlim set to some limit, or with Parallel.ForEachAsync and a MaxDegreeOfParallelism cap.

code.note: WhenAll is fastest but throttle it - 500 concurrent calls exhausts connections.

dialogue.text: I have 500 order IDs and need to enrich each from an API. I wrote a foreach with an await inside. Is that good?

It's correct but slow - each await waits for the previous call, so you're paying the sum of all 500 round-trips. Since the calls are independent, I'd start them and await Task.WhenAll so they run concurrently.
Okay, so let's fire all 500 at once with WhenAll. Any concern?
Yes - 500 simultaneous requests can trip the API's rate limits, exhaust the HTTP connection pool, and overload the downstream service. Unbounded concurrency is its own bug.
So how do you get speed without the blast radius?

I bound the concurrency - either a SemaphoreSlim with a limit like 10 around each call, or Parallel.ForEachAsync with MaxDegreeOfParallelism set. That keeps a steady pipeline without flooding the API.

followUpsQA.a: It serializes independent calls; total time is their sum.

followUpsQA.q: What does WhenAll improve and risk?
followUpsQA.a: Improves speed via concurrency; risks unbounded load on the API.
When each call depends on the previous result or order matters.

questions.q: A junior says "async makes my code multithreaded and faster." Correct them — and explain when threads actually help.

answer.oneLiner: I'd gently correct that, because async is really about concurrency, not parallelism. For I/O-bound work, awaiting actually frees up a thread while you wait, so you end up using fewer threads, not more. CPU-bound work gets no speedup from await at all; that's the situation where you actually want real parallelism by pushing the work onto threads with something like Task.Run or the Parallel class.
keyPoints: Async is not the same as multithreading, because an await on I/O uses no thread at all while it's waiting.
Concurrency means having many things in flight at once, while parallelism means many things actually executing at the same instant.
For I/O-bound work you want async and await, because it scales beautifully while using fewer threads.
For CPU-bound work you offload it onto threads with Task.Run or Parallel.For so you actually use multiple cores.

code.note: await ComputeExpensiveHash() wouldn't help - it's CPU work; Task.Run moves it off the caller.

dialogue.text: A junior on the team thinks adding async/await automatically makes code run on multiple threads and run faster. How do you set them straight?

Async is about concurrency, not parallelism. For I/O-bound work, await actually uses fewer threads - while waiting on the network or disk, no thread is consumed at all. It doesn't spin up threads or make CPU work faster.
So if I wrap a heavy calculation in an async method and await it, does that speed it up?
No - if it's pure CPU work, await does nothing useful; the calculation still runs on one core and you may just add overhead. await only pays off when there's real I/O to wait on.
Then how would you actually speed up that heavy calculation?

Use real parallelism - Task.Run to push it off the calling thread, or Parallel.For / PLINQ to split it across cores. That's parallelism for CPU-bound work, which is a different tool than async for I/O.

followUpsQA.q: Is async the same as multithreading?
followUpsQA.a: No - I/O await uses no thread while waiting; it's concurrency, not parallelism.
Concurrency is managing many tasks; parallelism is executing many at once.
To offload CPU-bound work off the current/UI thread.

followUpsQA.q: Best tool for I/O vs CPU?
followUpsQA.a: async/await for I/O-bound; Parallel/PLINQ for CPU-bound.

questions.q: Explain delegates, events, `Func`, and `Action` with examples.

answer.oneLiner: A delegate is basically a type-safe pointer to a method, so you can pass a method around like a variable and call it later. Func and Action are just ready-made generic delegates the framework gives you so you don't have to declare your own. An event is a wrapper around a delegate that locks things down, so outside code can subscribe and unsubscribe but can't actually fire it; for example a button exposes a Click event you can hook into but only the button itself raises it.
keyPoints: Action is for methods that return nothing, while Func is for methods that return a value, with the last type argument being the return type.
Delegates are multicast, so plus equals adds a method to the invocation list and minus equals removes one.
The event keyword lets outside code only add or remove handlers, never invoke the delegate itself.
Events conventionally follow the EventHandler of TEventArgs pattern for their signature.

code.note: ?.Invoke() is the thread-safe way to raise events (no TOCTOU race)

redFlags: Not knowing events block external invocation.
Event leaks from never unsubscribing with `-=`.

dialogue.text: Explain delegates, events, Func and Action.
A delegate is a type-safe function pointer. `Action` returns void, `Func` returns a value. An `event` wraps a delegate so outside code can only `+=`/`-=`, not invoke it.
Why wrap a delegate in an event?
Encapsulation - subscribers can subscribe and unsubscribe but can't raise the event or clear other subscribers. Only the declaring type can invoke it.
What's the classic event bug?
Memory leaks - if a subscriber never unsubscribes with `-=`, the publisher keeps it alive. And raise with `?.Invoke()` to avoid a null/TOCTOU race.

followUpsQA.a: An event blocks external invocation and reassignment.
Action returns void, Func returns a value, Predicate returns bool.

questions.q: What are generics in C#? Why are they useful and how are they different from Java generics?

answer.oneLiner: Generics let you write code once and reuse it with different types while still being fully type-safe, like a List that can be a list of ints or a list of strings without you rewriting it. The big difference from Java is that Java erases the type at runtime, whereas C# keeps it, so a List of int genuinely stores ints with no boxing into objects. That makes C# generics faster for value types and means the type information is still there at runtime.
keyPoints: You get real type safety, so a List of int simply won't let you put a string into it.
There's no boxing, because a List of int actually uses an int array under the hood rather than objects.
You can constrain the type parameter, for example saying where T is a class, has a parameterless constructor, and implements IComparable.
The types are reified, so at runtime typeof List of int is genuinely a different type from typeof List of string.

code.note: Generic repository with constraints — T must be a class and implement IEntity

redFlags: Not knowing C# generics avoid boxing for value types.
Using `object` instead of generics in new code.

dialogue.text: What are generics and why useful?
They give type-safe, reusable code parameterized by type - `List<int>` can't hold a string, and there's no casting.
How do they differ from Java generics?
C# reifies them - the type is preserved at runtime and specialized per value type, so `List<int>` uses `int[]` with no boxing. Java erases generics and boxes value types.
What can constraints do?
They restrict the type parameter - `where T : class, IEntity, new()` - so you can call interface members or `new T()` inside the generic.

followUpsQA.q: Do C# generics box value types?

followUpsQA.a: out for outputs, in for inputs.

followUpsQA.q: Are List<int> and List<string> the same type?
followUpsQA.a: No - reified generics make them distinct.

questions.q: Explain LINQ — key concepts and most commonly used operators.

answer.oneLiner: LINQ is a single, consistent way to write queries over collections in C#, whether that's an in-memory list, a database, or XML, all using the same operators like Where, Select, GroupBy, and OrderBy. The nice part is it's type-safe and composable, so you can chain those operators together to build up a query step by step. When you run it against a database through IQueryable, those same operators get translated into SQL for you.
keyPoints: LINQ is lazy, so the query doesn't actually run until you enumerate it with something like ToList or a foreach.
There's method syntax and query syntax, and the two are equivalent so you can use whichever reads better.
The core operators you'll use most are Where, Select, SelectMany, GroupBy, OrderBy, Any, and First.
When you query IQueryable, like with EF Core, the operators get translated all the way down into SQL.

code.snippet:
var result = orders
    .Where(o => o.Status == OrderStatus.Shipped)
    .GroupBy(o => o.CustomerId)
    .Select(g => new {
        CustomerId = g.Key,
        TotalSpend = g.Sum(o => o.Total),
        OrderCount = g.Count()
    })
    .OrderByDescending(x => x.TotalSpend)
    .Take(10)
    .ToList();
code.note: Top 10 customers by spend — if orders is IQueryable, this becomes a single SQL query

redFlags: Not knowing LINQ is lazy — re-enumerating the source.
`.ToList()` before a `Where` on a DbSet.

dialogue.text: Explain LINQ and its key operators.
LINQ is a unified, composable query syntax over `IEnumerable`/`IQueryable` - operators like `Where`, `Select`, `GroupBy`, `OrderBy`, `Any`, `First`.
What's the catch with deferred execution?
Queries are lazy - they run when enumerated, not when defined. So re-enumerating re-runs the query, and mutating the source before enumeration affects results.
On EF Core, what's different?
On `IQueryable` the operators build an expression tree that translates to SQL, so filtering happens in the database, not in memory.

followUpsQA.a: The query runs when enumerated, not when written.
First throws if empty; FirstOrDefault returns default.

questions.q: Explain pattern matching in C# — switch expressions, type patterns, property patterns.

answer.oneLiner: Pattern matching lets you check a value's type, its shape, and its properties all in one short, readable expression, instead of writing long chains of if-else statements with casts. For example you can check whether something is a Circle and grab it as a typed variable in the same line. It really cleans up code where you'd otherwise be doing a bunch of type checks and casting by hand.
keyPoints: With a type pattern you can write if shape is Circle c, which both checks the type and gives you a typed variable at once.
A switch expression returns a value directly and can be made exhaustive so every case is covered.
A property pattern lets you match on a value's properties, like shape is Circle with a Radius greater than 10.
Relational patterns let you check ranges, like x is greater than 0 and less than 100.

code.note: Switch expression: exhaustive, returns a value, guards with 'when'

dialogue.text: Explain pattern matching in C#.
It tests a value's type, shape, and properties in one expression - type patterns, switch expressions, property patterns, and relational patterns - replacing verbose if/else chains.
Show me a property pattern.
`shape is Circle { Radius: > 10 }` matches a Circle with radius over 10 in one line, and you can bind it: `{ Radius: var r }`.
What makes a switch expression nice?
It returns a value and the compiler warns if it isn't exhaustive, and you can add guards with `when`.

followUpsQA.a: A type with a Deconstruct method or a record.

followUpsQA.q: How do C# 11 list patterns work?

followUpsQA.a: Expression returns a value and is exhaustive.

questions.q: What are extension methods and when should you use them?

answer.oneLiner: Extension methods let you add new methods to a type that already exists, without changing that type or making a subclass. You write a static method in a static class where the first parameter starts with the keyword this, and then you can call it as if it were built into the type, like calling Truncate on a string. A really common example is LINQ, where everything like Where and Select is actually just an extension method on collections.
keyPoints: Even though it's a static method, you call it like a normal instance method, so something like "Hello".Truncate(5) just works.
It's really just compile-time syntactic sugar, because the compiler rewrites your call into a plain static method call behind the scenes.
They're great for building fluent, chainable APIs and for adding behavior to sealed types or third-party types you can't modify.
They can't reach private fields of the type, so if your method behaves in a non-obvious way you should document that clearly.

code.note: Extension methods must be in a static class

dialogue.text: What are extension methods?
Static methods in a static class with a `this`-qualified first parameter - they let you call them like instance methods on existing types without modifying them.
How are they actually resolved?
It's compile-time sugar - the call is rewritten to a plain static method call. There's no runtime magic.
Any limitations?
They only see public members, so no access to private state, and an instance method with the same signature always wins over an extension.

questions.q: What are `yield return` and iterators in C#?

answer.oneLiner: yield return lets you produce a sequence of values one at a time, lazily, without ever building the whole collection in memory first. When you use it, the compiler quietly turns your method into a state machine that implements an enumerator for you. Think of it like a vending machine that only hands you the next item when you actually ask for it, instead of dumping everything out at once.
keyPoints: A method that uses yield has to return either IEnumerable of T or IEnumerator of T.
Execution pauses at each yield return and only picks back up where it left off when the caller asks for the next item with MoveNext.
You can use yield break to stop the sequence early and finish iterating.
It's perfect for infinite or streamed sequences and for lazy pipelines where you don't want to compute everything up front.

code.note: Infinite sequence — Take(10) pulls only 10 values; the rest are never computed

dialogue.text: What is yield return?
It produces a lazily-evaluated sequence - the compiler generates an `IEnumerator<T>` state machine that suspends at each `yield return` and resumes on `MoveNext()`.
What's the benefit over building a list?
You don't materialize everything - you can stream, model infinite sequences, and the consumer decides when to stop with something like `Take(10)`.
How do you exit early?
`yield break` ends the iterator. And note the body doesn't run at all until the first `MoveNext()` - it's fully lazy.

followUpsQA.q: When does the body first run?
followUpsQA.a: On the first MoveNext - it's deferred.

questions.q: What are C# records and how do they differ from classes?

answer.oneLiner: Records, which came in C# 9, are types designed to hold data, and they give you value-based equality, easy copying, and very compact syntax out of the box. Instead of comparing by reference like a normal class, two records are equal when their contents match, which is exactly what you want for things like DTOs and value objects. You also get a with expression to make a tweaked copy without mutating the original.
keyPoints: The compiler automatically generates value equality for you, so ==, Equals, and GetHashCode all compare the actual data rather than the reference.
The with expression makes a copy of the record while changing just the one property you specify, leaving the original untouched.
You can have a record class, which is a reference type, or a record struct, which is a value type.
With positional syntax the compiler generates the constructor, the properties, and a deconstructor for you automatically.

code.note: Records auto-generate Equals/GetHashCode based on all properties

dialogue.text: What are records and how do they differ from classes?
Records (C# 9) are types with built-in value equality, a `with` expression for non-destructive copies, and concise positional syntax - ideal for DTOs and value objects.
What does value equality mean here?
Two records with the same property values are `==` equal; the compiler generates `Equals` and `GetHashCode` from all properties. A class compares by reference.
record class vs record struct?
`record class` is a reference type with value equality; `record struct` is a value type. Both get the generated equality and deconstruction.

followUpsQA.a: Copies the record changing some properties.
Yes - record from record (not from a plain class with equality).
record struct adds value equality and with-expressions.

questions.q: What are tuples in C# and when should you use them?

answer.oneLiner: Value tuples, added in C# 7, let a method return several values at once without you having to create a whole separate class just to bundle them. You can give the elements names, like Name and Age, and then unpack them into separate variables on the receiving side. It's a quick, lightweight way to return more than one thing from a method.
keyPoints: The newer ValueTuple is a struct, which is lighter weight, whereas the old Tuple type was a class.
You can name the elements, so a method can return something like a First and Last pair and the names show up at the call site.
You can deconstruct a tuple straight into separate variables, for example var with first and last on the left of GetName.
Tuples are best for internal or private methods, but for a public API you're usually better off with a record or a class.

dialogue.text: What are tuples and when do you use them?
C# 7 value tuples return multiple values without a new type - `(string Name, int Age)` with named elements and deconstruction.
Old Tuple vs ValueTuple?
The old `Tuple` is a class with `Item1`/`Item2`; `ValueTuple` is a struct, lighter, and supports named fields and deconstruction.
When wouldn't you use a tuple?
For public APIs - a named record or class is clearer and self-documenting. Tuples are best for internal, local return values.

questions.q: What is the difference between `ref`, `out`, and `in` parameters?

answer.oneLiner: These three keywords all pass arguments by reference instead of by copy, but they differ in direction. ref means the method can both read and write the caller's variable, out is write-only so the method is required to assign it before returning, and in is a read-only reference you use mainly to avoid copying a big struct. So ref is two-way, out is for handing values back, and in is purely for performance on large data.
keyPoints: With ref the caller has to initialize the variable first, and then the method can read it and write to it.
With out the method is required to assign a value before it returns, which is exactly the pattern you see in things like TryParse.
With in you're passing a read-only reference, so a large struct doesn't get copied when it's handed in.
You can also use ref returns and ref locals to hand back a reference to something and avoid copying it.

code.note: `in` is important for large readonly structs in performance-sensitive code

dialogue.text: ref vs out vs in parameters?
`ref` passes a variable by reference for read/write, `out` is write-only and the callee must assign it, and `in` is a read-only reference to avoid copying large structs.
What's the classic out use?
The `TryParse` pattern - `int.TryParse(s, out var n)` returns a bool and writes the result via `out`.
Why does `in` exist?
To pass a large readonly struct by reference without copying it, while guaranteeing the callee can't modify it.

followUpsQA.a: ref needs caller-initialized; out must be assigned by callee.
Large readonly structs in hot paths.
Returning a reference to avoid a copy.

questions.q: What are nullable reference types (NRT) and how do you enable and use them?

answer.oneLiner: Nullable reference types, introduced in C# 8 and turned on with the Nullable enable setting, give you compile-time help to avoid null mistakes. Once it's on, a type written as string with a question mark is allowed to be null, while a plain string is assumed to never be null, and the compiler warns you if you dereference something that might be null. It doesn't change anything at runtime, it just helps you catch bugs while you're still writing the code.
keyPoints: You opt into it per project by setting the Nullable flag in the csproj file.
The compiler uses flow analysis to follow your code and figure out where a value could or couldn't be null.
Attributes like NotNull, MaybeNull, and NotNullWhen let you fine-tune the nullability contract on tricky methods.
The runtime behavior is exactly the same as before, and you can migrate gradually one file at a time using a nullable enable directive.

dialogue.text: What are nullable reference types and how do you enable them?
C# 8 feature via `<Nullable>enable</Nullable>` - `string?` allows null, `string` is assumed non-null, and the compiler warns on risky dereferences.
Does it change runtime behavior?
No - it's compile-time flow analysis only. A `string?` is still just a reference at runtime; nothing is enforced by the CLR.
How do you migrate a big project?
Enable per-file with `#nullable enable`, fix warnings incrementally, and use attributes like `[NotNullWhen]` to express precise contracts.

questions.q: What are expression-bodied members and when do you use them?

answer.oneLiner: Expression-bodied members let you use the arrow syntax instead of a full curly-brace block when a member is just a single expression. So instead of writing a method body with return, you write the arrow and the expression, and it works for methods, properties, constructors, and operators. It mainly just cuts down on boilerplate and makes simple members easier to read.
keyPoints: It works on lots of member kinds, including methods, properties, constructors, indexers, and operators.
A property can be written compactly, like a FullName property that returns the first and last name combined with the arrow syntax.
You can use it for overrides too, such as an arrow-bodied ToString.
You should only use it when the member is a single readable expression, otherwise a normal block is clearer.

code.note: Constructor and properties all expression-bodied — very concise

dialogue.text: What are expression-bodied members?
They use `=>` instead of a block for single-expression members - methods, properties, constructors, operators - cutting boilerplate.
Where can you use them?
Methods, read-only properties, indexers, constructors, even operators - `public string FullName => $"{First} {Last}";`.
When shouldn't you?
When the body isn't a single readable expression - multi-statement logic is clearer in a block.

questions.q: What is covariance and contravariance in C# generics?

answer.oneLiner: Covariance and contravariance are about when you're allowed to substitute one generic type for another. Covariance, marked with out T, lets you treat a more specific type as a more general one, so an IEnumerable of Dog can be used where an IEnumerable of Animal is expected. Contravariance, marked with in T, is the opposite and applies to inputs, where a thing that accepts a general type can stand in for one that accepts a more specific type.
keyPoints: Variance only works on interfaces and delegates, not on regular classes.
The out T marker makes a type parameter covariant, and it can only appear in output positions like return values.
The in T marker makes a type parameter contravariant, and it can only appear in input positions like method parameters.
An easy way to remember it is that producers, which hand values out, use out, and consumers, which take values in, use in.

code.note: Variance only applies to reference types — not value types

dialogue.text: Covariance and contravariance in generics?
Covariance (`out T`) lets a more-derived type stand in for a less-derived one - `IEnumerable<Dog>` as `IEnumerable<Animal>`. Contravariance (`in T`) is the reverse, for inputs.
What's the mnemonic?
Producers are `out`, consumers are `in`. `out` types only appear in output positions; `in` types only in input positions.
Does it work on classes?
No - only on interfaces and delegates, and only for reference types, not value types.

questions.q: What is `dynamic` in C# and when would you (and wouldn't you) use it?

answer.oneLiner: The dynamic keyword tells the compiler to skip type checking for that variable and instead figure out its members at runtime using the Dynamic Language Runtime. That gives you flexibility but you lose all the safety nets, so any mistake shows up as a runtime error instead of a build error. You should reach for it only in narrow cases like talking to old COM components or handling genuinely dynamic data.
keyPoints: You get no IntelliSense and no compile-time checking, so any errors only surface when the code actually runs.
Legitimate uses include COM interop, working with ExpandoObject, and dealing with loosely-shaped dynamic JSON.
In normal application code you should avoid it and prefer something like System.Text.Json with proper model classes.
It's also slower than normal static dispatch because the member lookup happens at runtime.

dialogue.text: What is `dynamic` and when would you use it?
`dynamic` skips compile-time type checking and resolves members at runtime via the DLR - no IntelliSense, errors surface at runtime.
Where is it legitimately useful?
COM interop, `ExpandoObject`, and loosely-typed dynamic data. Otherwise I avoid it in app code.
Why avoid it generally?
You lose compile-time safety and tooling, and it's slower than static dispatch - prefer typed models or `System.Text.Json`.

followUpsQA.a: var is statically typed inferred; dynamic defers to runtime.

questions.q: Explain the IOptions pattern: IOptions vs IOptionsSnapshot vs IOptionsMonitor.

answer.oneLiner: The options pattern is the standard way in .NET to take a section of your configuration and bind it to a strongly-typed class that you inject through dependency injection. The three interfaces all give you that class, but they differ in how they handle config that changes while the app is running. The key distinction is when, if ever, each one picks up updated values.
keyPoints: IOptions of T is registered as a singleton, reads the value once, and never refreshes it after that.
IOptionsSnapshot of T is scoped, so you get a fresh value computed per request, which is handy in web apps.
IOptionsMonitor of T is a singleton that supports live reloading and even gives you an OnChange callback when the config updates.
You wire it all up by calling services.Configure of T and pointing it at the right config section.

code.note: Use IOptionsSnapshot in scoped services that need reloaded config

dialogue.text: Explain IOptions vs IOptionsSnapshot vs IOptionsMonitor.

All bind a config section to a typed class via DI. `IOptions<T>` is a singleton read once; `IOptionsSnapshot<T>` is scoped and fresh per request; `IOptionsMonitor<T>` is a singleton with live reload and an `OnChange` callback.
Why can't you inject IOptionsSnapshot into a singleton?
It's scoped - injecting it into a singleton would be a captive dependency. Use `IOptionsMonitor` in singletons for reloadable config.
How do you validate options at startup?
`services.AddOptions<T>().Bind(...).ValidateDataAnnotations().ValidateOnStart()` so bad config fails fast.

followUpsQA.a: Scoped IOptionsSnapshot in a singleton is captive.

questions.q: What is a partial class (and partial method) in C#?

answer.oneLiner: The partial keyword lets you split a single class, or even a single method, across several files, and the compiler stitches them back together into one type at build time. The main reason it exists is to keep machine-generated code separate from the code you write by hand. For example a designer or a source generator can own one file while you safely edit your own logic in another.
keyPoints: Every piece of the type has to be marked partial and they all have to agree on the same accessibility level.
It's commonly used for generated code, like what you get from Entity Framework, Razor, or source generators.
With partial methods you put the declaration in one part and an optional body in another part.
If a partial method never gets a body, the compiler simply removes any calls to it so there's no leftover cost.

code.note: Both files compile into one Customer type

dialogue.text: What is a partial class?
`partial` splits one class across multiple files, merged by the compiler into one type - mainly to separate generated code from hand-written code.
Where is it commonly used?
Designer/generated code - EF models, Razor, WinForms designers, and source generators - so regeneration doesn't clobber your code.
What about partial methods?
You declare the signature in one part; if no part provides a body, the compiler removes the declaration and all calls to it - a zero-cost hook.

followUpsQA.q: What if a partial method has no body?
followUpsQA.a: Calls are removed by the compiler.
Yes - they emit a partial part.

questions.q: What is the difference between `throw` and `throw ex`?

answer.oneLiner: The difference is all about the stack trace, which is the breadcrumb trail showing where an error came from. Writing throw on its own rethrows the current exception and keeps that original trail intact, while throw ex resets the trail to the current line and hides where the problem actually started. So in almost every case you want plain throw, because losing the original location makes debugging much harder.
keyPoints: Using throw by itself preserves the original throw point, and this is the version you almost always want.
Using throw ex overwrites the stack trace with the current line, which throws away valuable debugging information, so avoid it.
If you actually want to wrap the error, throw a new exception and pass the original one in as the inner exception.
You can also use ExceptionDispatchInfo.Capture followed by Throw to preserve the stack trace across boundaries like async or thread handoffs.

code.note: Always prefer bare 'throw;' when rethrowing

redFlags: Using `throw ex;` and losing the origin of the bug.

dialogue.text: throw vs throw ex?
`throw;` rethrows preserving the original stack trace; `throw ex;` resets it to the current line, hiding where the error actually originated.
So which do you use and why?
Always bare `throw;` when rethrowing - keeping the original throw point is essential for debugging.
How do you rethrow across threads or boundaries?
`ExceptionDispatchInfo.Capture(ex).Throw()` preserves the stack trace. To add context, wrap with `throw new MyException(msg, ex)` using the inner exception.

followUpsQA.a: It loses the origin of the bug.

questions.q: What is the difference between `const`, `readonly`, and `static readonly`?

answer.oneLiner: These three all give you values that don't change, but they differ in when and how they're set. A const is a true compile-time constant whose value gets baked directly into whoever uses it, a readonly field is set once at runtime in the constructor and can differ per instance, and a static readonly is one shared value for the whole type that's also set at runtime. So const is fixed at compile time, while the readonly variants are computed when the program runs.
keyPoints: A const is implicitly static, evaluated at compile time, and limited to primitives and strings.
Because a const value gets inlined into other assemblies that reference it, changing it can cause versioning problems unless everything is recompiled.
A readonly field can be assigned at its declaration or in the constructor, and its value can be different for each instance.
A static readonly field is set once for the whole type and can hold any computed value, not just simple literals.

code.snippet:
public const double Pi = 3.14159;        // compile-time, inlined
public static readonly DateTime Start = DateTime.UtcNow; // runtime
public readonly int Id;                   // per-instance, set in ctor
public Thing(int id) => Id = id;
code.note: Use static readonly (not const) for values that may change across versions

dialogue.text: const vs readonly vs static readonly?
`const` is a compile-time constant inlined into callers. `readonly` is set once at runtime, per instance, in the ctor. `static readonly` is one shared runtime value for the type.
Why can a public const be a breaking change?
Its value is baked into referencing assemblies at compile time. If you change it but don't recompile the consumers, they keep the old value.
So what's the safer choice for a shared value?
`static readonly` - it's read at runtime, so consumers always see the current value, and it can hold computed or non-primitive values.

followUpsQA.a: static readonly - const is primitives/strings only.
Per-instance value set in the constructor.

questions.q: What is the difference between First() and FirstOrDefault()?

answer.oneLiner: Both of these grab the first matching item from a sequence, but they behave differently when nothing matches. First throws an InvalidOperationException if there's no matching element, whereas FirstOrDefault just hands you the default value instead, which is null for reference types and zero for an int. So you pick based on whether an empty result is a real error or a perfectly normal case.
keyPoints: Use First when you genuinely expect an element to be there, because it throws if there isn't one.
Use FirstOrDefault when an empty result is acceptable, since it returns the default value rather than throwing.
The same pairing applies to Single and SingleOrDefault, and to Last and LastOrDefault.
Single is stricter still, because it also throws if more than one element matches.

code.note: FirstOrDefault returns null for classes — guard against NRE afterward

dialogue.text: First vs FirstOrDefault?
`First()` throws `InvalidOperationException` if nothing matches; `FirstOrDefault()` returns the type's default - null for reference types, 0 for int.
When do you pick each?
`First` when an element must exist and absence is a bug. `FirstOrDefault` when empty is a valid case - then guard the result against null.
What about Single?
`Single` also throws if more than one matches - use it when exactly one is expected, like a lookup by primary key.

followUpsQA.a: Single throws if more than one match.

followUpsQA.q: Is this LINQ deferred or immediate?

questions.q: Compare IEnumerable, ICollection, IList, and IQueryable.

answer.oneLiner: These interfaces form a ladder of capability where each one adds more than the last. IEnumerable just lets you iterate, ICollection adds things like counting and adding and removing, and IList adds index-based access on top of that. IQueryable is a bit different, it's like IEnumerable but it carries an expression tree so the query can be translated and run somewhere else, like turned into SQL and executed on a database.
keyPoints: IEnumerable of T only supports forward iteration, it's lazy, and it doesn't even give you a Count.
ICollection of T builds on that by adding Count along with Add, Remove, and Contains.
IList of T goes further and adds index access through the indexer plus the ability to Insert at a position.
IQueryable of T is IEnumerable plus an expression tree, which is what makes things like LINQ-to-SQL possible.

dialogue.text: Compare IEnumerable, ICollection, IList, IQueryable.
They're a capability hierarchy: IEnumerable iterates, ICollection adds Count/Add/Remove, IList adds an indexer and Insert. IQueryable is IEnumerable plus an expression tree for SQL.
Which should a method return?
Return the least specific that the caller needs - usually `IEnumerable<T>` or `IReadOnlyList<T>` - for flexibility and to avoid exposing mutation.
Why does Count() on IEnumerable enumerate everything?
IEnumerable has no Count property, so LINQ's `Count()` walks the whole sequence. ICollection exposes a Count property that's O(1).

questions.q: What is a global using directive (C# 10)?

answer.oneLiner: A global using, added in C# 10, lets you declare a using directive one single time and have it apply across your entire project. That means you stop repeating the same imports at the top of every file. It's a small thing, but it really cleans up the clutter in large projects where the same namespaces show up everywhere.
keyPoints: You put a global using, like global using System.Linq, in any one file, and people often keep them in a dedicated GlobalUsings file.
Turning on implicit usings with the ImplicitUsings setting automatically adds the common namespaces for your project type.
You can combine it with global using static and with aliases for even more convenience.
It's best to keep all of them in one central file so they're easy to find and manage.

code.note: Applies to all files in the compilation

dialogue.text: What is a global using directive?
C# 10 - `global using` declares a using once for the whole project, removing repeated imports from every file.
How does it combine with implicit usings?
`<ImplicitUsings>enable</ImplicitUsings>` auto-adds common namespaces; you add your own with `global using` in a central file.
Best practice for organizing them?
Keep all `global using` lines in one file like GlobalUsings.cs for discoverability, and use `global using static` or aliases where helpful.

questions.q: What causes a NullReferenceException ('Object reference not set to an instance of an object') and how do you avoid it?

answer.oneLiner: A NullReferenceException happens when you try to use a member, like calling a method or reading a property, on a reference that's actually null, meaning it doesn't point to any object. It's basically asking an object that isn't there to do something. You avoid it by checking for null first, or by using helpers like the null-conditional operator, the null-coalescing operator, and nullable reference types to catch the risk earlier.
keyPoints: The cause is accessing a member on a null object, which can come from an uninitialized field, an empty query result, and similar situations.
The null-conditional operator, written as question-mark-dot, short-circuits to null instead of throwing when the thing on the left is null.
The null-coalescing operators let you supply a fallback value when something is null.
Turning on nullable reference types gets the compiler to warn you about these risks at build time instead of you finding out at runtime.

code.snippet:
string? name = GetName();
int len = name?.Length ?? 0;     // safe
name ??= "unknown";              // assign if null
if (name is not null) Use(name); // explicit guard
code.note: Most NREs come from method results that can return null (e.g. FirstOrDefault)

redFlags: Catching NullReferenceException instead of fixing the null.
Sprinkling `!` (null-forgiving) to silence warnings.

dialogue.text: What causes a NullReferenceException?
Dereferencing - calling a member on - a reference that's null. Common sources are uninitialized fields and methods like `FirstOrDefault` returning null.
How do you avoid it?
Null-conditional `?.`, null-coalescing `??`/`??=` for fallbacks, explicit guards, and enabling nullable reference types so the compiler warns at build.
What's the wrong way to handle it?
Catching `NullReferenceException` or spraying `!` to silence warnings - that hides the bug instead of fixing the null.

questions.q: Are null and an empty string the same thing in C#?

answer.oneLiner: No, they're not the same thing. null means there's no string object there at all, while an empty string, which is the same as string.Empty, is a real, valid string that just happens to have zero characters. When you want to treat both of those cases the same way, you use string.IsNullOrEmpty to check for them together.
keyPoints: A null reference has no instance behind it, so trying to read its Length throws a NullReferenceException.
An empty string and string.Empty are the same interned empty instance, and its Length is zero.
string.IsNullOrEmpty checks for both the null case and the empty case in one call.
string.IsNullOrWhiteSpace goes a step further and also treats strings that are only spaces or tabs as empty.

code.note: Prefer string.IsNullOrWhiteSpace for user input validation

dialogue.text: Are null and an empty string the same?
No - `null` means no string object at all, while `""` is a valid zero-length string. `""` and `string.Empty` are the same instance.
How do you check both safely?
`string.IsNullOrEmpty(s)` covers null and empty; `string.IsNullOrWhiteSpace(s)` also treats spaces and tabs as empty - best for user input.
What happens if you call .Length on null?
A NullReferenceException - whereas `"".Length` is just 0.

questions.q: Can an enum use inheritance in C#?

answer.oneLiner: No, you can't use inheritance with enums. Every enum automatically derives from System.Enum and is effectively sealed, so you can't make one enum inherit from another or extend an existing enum with extra values. If you need that kind of extensibility, you have to reach for a different approach instead of trying to inherit.
keyPoints: Enums are sealed value types, so you can't write one enum inheriting from another enum.
The only thing you can specify is the underlying integral type, for example declaring an enum backed by a byte.
If you want to add behavior, you can use extension methods or build a smart-enum class instead.
The Flags attribute only enables combining values with bitwise operations, it has nothing to do with inheritance.

dialogue.text: Can an enum use inheritance?
No - every enum implicitly derives from `System.Enum` and is effectively sealed, so you can't inherit from one or add values by subclassing.
What can you customize then?
Only the underlying integral type, like `enum E : byte`. To extend behavior you use extension methods or a smart-enum class pattern.
What does [Flags] add?
It signals the enum is a bit field for bitwise combinations - it's not inheritance, just OR-able values.

followUpsQA.q: Can you change the backing type?

questions.q: Explain the ternary operator and the null-coalescing operators (?? and ??=).

answer.oneLiner: These are all shortcuts for working with conditions and nulls. The ternary operator, written as cond ? a : b, just picks between two values based on a true or false check, so it's like a one-line if-else. The null-coalescing operator a ?? b gives you a unless a happens to be null, in which case it falls back to b, which is handy for default values. And a ??= b is a small variation that only assigns b into a when a is currently null, so you don't overwrite a value that's already there.
keyPoints: The ternary operator works on any boolean condition, while ?? specifically checks whether something is null.
The ?? operator is right-associative and you can chain it, so x ?? y ?? z walks through each one until it finds something that isn't null.
The ??= operator is just the compound assignment version of ??, meaning it only assigns when the left side is null.
It pairs really nicely with the safe-navigation operator, so something like obj?.Name ?? "N/A" reads as give me the name, but say N/A if either the object or the name is null.

code.snippet:
int max = a > b ? a : b;          // ternary
string name = input ?? "default"; // null-coalescing
cache ??= LoadFromDb();           // assign only if null
code.note: ?? and ??= only react to null, not to false/empty

dialogue.text: Explain the ternary and null-coalescing operators.
`cond ? a : b` picks one of two values by a boolean. `a ?? b` returns `a` unless it's null. `a ??= b` assigns `b` to `a` only if `a` is null.
How do ?? and ternary differ?
Ternary reacts to any boolean condition; `??` reacts specifically to null - not to false or empty.
Can you chain them?
Yes - `??` is right-associative: `x ?? y ?? z` returns the first non-null. It pairs nicely with `?.`: `obj?.Name ?? "N/A"`.

followUpsQA.a: Assigns only if the left is null.

questions.q: What is the difference between early binding and late binding?

answer.oneLiner: Early binding, also called static binding, means the compiler figures out which method or property you're calling while it's compiling your code, so you get full type checking up front. Late binding means that decision is delayed until the program is actually running, usually through the dynamic keyword or reflection. So early binding is like knowing the address before you leave the house, while late binding is figuring it out once you're already on the road. You'd lean on late binding when you genuinely don't know the type ahead of time, like working with plugins or COM objects.
keyPoints: With early binding you get compile-time checks, IntelliSense support, and faster execution because everything is resolved before the program runs.
With late binding the method is resolved at runtime, typically through the dynamic keyword or something like Type.InvokeMember.
The downside of late binding is that any mistakes only show up at runtime instead of being caught by the compiler.
Late binding is the right choice for things like COM interop, plugin systems, or data whose shape you don't know in advance.

code.snippet:
var s = "hi"; s.ToUpper();        // early binding
dynamic d = "hi"; d.ToUpper();    // late binding (runtime resolve)
d.NoSuchMethod();                 // compiles, throws at runtime
code.note: Virtual dispatch is still 'early bound' (compile-time known signature)

dialogue.text: Early binding vs late binding?
Early (static) binding resolves members at compile time with full type checking. Late binding resolves them at runtime via `dynamic` or reflection.
What's the trade-off?
Early binding gives compile-time safety, IntelliSense, and speed. Late binding is flexible for COM, plugins, or unknown-shape data but errors surface only at runtime.
Is virtual dispatch late binding?
No - it's still early bound; the signature is known at compile time, only the concrete override is chosen at runtime.

questions.q: How do you add new methods to a sealed class you cannot modify?

answer.oneLiner: The trick here is extension methods. A sealed class can't be inherited, so subclassing it is off the table, but you can write a static method that takes the class as its first parameter and it looks and feels like a regular instance method on that type. So even though I can't open up the class itself, I can bolt new behavior onto it from the outside. This is exactly how LINQ adds all those handy methods to collections.
keyPoints: Because sealed blocks inheritance, you simply can't subclass the type to add methods.
An extension method is a static method whose first parameter is marked with this, and it has to live inside a static class.
The catch is that it can only see the public members of the class, so it has no access to any private internal state.
All of LINQ works exactly this way, extending sealed types and interfaces without ever modifying them.

code.note: Extension methods are the standard answer for extending sealed/third-party types

dialogue.text: How do you add methods to a sealed class you can't modify?
Extension methods - a static method with a `this` first parameter in a static class. You can't subclass a sealed type, but you can extend it this way.
What are the limits?
Extensions only see public members - no private state. And an instance method always wins over an extension with the same signature.
A real example?
All of LINQ extends `IEnumerable<T>` and sealed types like `string` this way - `"a@b.com".IsEmail()` via an extension.

questions.q: Is the index accessible inside a foreach loop?
