Yes. The compiler synthesises IEquatable<Point> implementation alongside the == operator and GetHashCode override, making it suitable as dictionary keys without boxing.

followUpsQA.q: What is the difference between record struct equality and plain struct's default Equals?
followUpsQA.a: Plain struct's default Equals uses reflection to compare fields, which is slow and can have edge cases with float NaN. record struct's synthesised Equals is strongly typed and field-by-field without reflection.

answer.oneLiner: This won't compile at all. A plain struct doesn't get an == operator for free, so trying to use == on two of them triggers compiler error CS0019, saying operator == can't be applied to operands of type Point. If you want to compare them with ==, you either have to write the operator yourself or switch to a record struct, which generates it automatically.

code.note: Unlike record struct, plain struct does NOT synthesise == and !=. You must overload them manually or use Equals().

dialogue.text: Does this compile? Many developers assume it prints True.

No — CS0019: Operator '==' cannot be applied to operands of type 'Point' and 'Point'. Plain struct does not generate == or !=. This is a common gotcha, especially for developers coming from languages where value types automatically support equality operators.
How do you fix it while keeping Point a plain struct?

Override operator == and != manually, and also override Equals and GetHashCode for consistency: public static bool operator ==(Point l, Point r) => l.X == r.X && l.Y == r.Y; public static bool operator !=(Point l, Point r) => !(l == r); Then also override Equals and GetHashCode.
What is the easiest way to get == for free on a value type?
Declare it as 'record struct Point(int X, int Y)'. The compiler synthesises a correct, strongly-typed == operator and all related equality members automatically.

followUpsQA.q: Why doesn't C# auto-generate == for plain struct like it does for record struct?
followUpsQA.a: Historical design decision. The runtime-provided Equals uses reflection and has edge cases (float NaN, sub-typed fields). C# chose not to synthesise == silently to avoid surprising behaviour; you must opt in explicitly.

followUpsQA.q: Does plain struct support != by default?
followUpsQA.a: No, for the same reason — != is also not synthesised. And if you define == you MUST also define != (the compiler enforces this as a pair).

followUpsQA.q: Can you use a plain struct as a Dictionary key safely?
followUpsQA.a: Yes if you override GetHashCode and Equals. The default GetHashCode for struct uses the first non-null field's hash, which can cause collisions and is unstable across .NET versions. Always override both.

answer.oneLiner: This prints 60. The stackalloc keyword carves out room for three integers directly on the stack, and the Span gives you a safe, bounds-checked way to read and write that memory. After filling in 10, 20, and 30, the loop just adds them up to 60. The nice part is you get fast stack allocation without resorting to unsafe pointer code.

code.note: Span<int> is a ref struct — it cannot escape to the heap. No 'unsafe' block required when assigning stackalloc directly to Span<T>.

dialogue.text: What does this print and why use stackalloc?

It prints 60. stackalloc carves 12 bytes on the current stack frame for 3 ints. Span<int> wraps that region with bounds-checked access. There is no heap allocation, no GC pressure, and the memory is automatically reclaimed when the scope exits.
Why is Span<T> a ref struct and what does that restrict?

A ref struct can only live on the stack. This prevents it from being boxed, stored in a field of a regular class, captured in a lambda, or passed to an async method. These restrictions ensure the span cannot outlive the stack memory it points to, which would be a dangling pointer.
Do you need the unsafe keyword for stackalloc with Span<T>?
No — since C# 7.2, Span<int> span = stackalloc int[3] is allowed in a safe context. You still need AllowUnsafeBlocks if you use raw pointers, but Span<T> stackalloc itself is safe.

followUpsQA.q: What happens if the stackalloc size is too large?
followUpsQA.a: A StackOverflowException is thrown at runtime, crashing the process. Unlike heap allocation, there is no catchable OutOfMemoryException. Typical safe limits are a few KB; use ArrayPool<T>.Shared for larger buffers.

followUpsQA.q: How does ReadOnlySpan<T> differ from Span<T>?
followUpsQA.a: ReadOnlySpan<T> provides read-only access to the underlying memory. Its indexer returns T by value not ref T. You cannot write through it. String.AsSpan() returns ReadOnlySpan<char>.

followUpsQA.q: Can you return a Span<T> from a method?
followUpsQA.a: Not if it wraps stackalloc memory — that would be a dangling reference. You can return a Span<T> wrapping an array or a MemoryMarshal slice that you know outlives the method.

answer.oneLiner: This prints 0 and then 2. Each Select in the LINQ chain takes in a copy of the struct, increments that copy, and hands back a new copy, so the original list is never touched and stays at zero. The result list, on the other hand, went through two increment stages, so it ends up at 2. It's a great example of how value-type copying interacts with LINQ pipelines.

code.note: LINQ's deferred pipeline passes structs by value at each step. No stage shares memory with the original collection. This is correct-looking code that silently leaves the source unchanged.

dialogue.text: Walk through exactly what value each variable holds at the end.

list[0].Value is 0 and result[0].Value is 2. The list is a List<Counter>, so IEnumerator<Counter>.Current returns a copy each time Select iterates. The first Select lambda increments its copy from 0 to 1 and returns it. The second Select lambda receives that value (1), increments to 2, and returns it. ToList materialises these copies into result. The original list[0] was never written to.
Would this behave differently if Counter were a class?

Yes. If Counter were a class, IEnumerator<Counter>.Current would return the same reference each iteration. Both Select lambdas would mutate the original heap object. list[0].Value would be 2 after ToList, and result[0] would reference the same object — also showing 2.
Is there a way to mutate a List<struct>'s elements through LINQ?

Not directly through standard LINQ — every step operates on copies. To mutate in place, use a for loop with the index-assign pattern, or obtain a Span<Counter> via CollectionsMarshal.AsSpan(list) and iterate by ref.

followUpsQA.q: What does 'deferred execution' mean in this context?
followUpsQA.a: The Select calls build lazy iterator chains; nothing executes until ToList enumerates them. Each element flows through the entire pipeline one at a time — struct copies are made per element per stage at that point.

followUpsQA.q: Could you accidentally fix this with AsParallel?
followUpsQA.a: No. PLINQ also works on copies of struct elements. The copies would be incremented on worker threads and collected into the result, but the original list would still be unmodified — and you'd add race conditions for no gain.

followUpsQA.q: How does this interact with closures capturing a struct?
followUpsQA.a: If a lambda closes over a local struct variable (rather than receiving it as a parameter), C# boxes the captured variable into a compiler-generated class. Mutations inside the closure affect the boxed copy, not the original local — another silent no-op trap.

questions.q: Explain the MVC architecture and how a request flows through Model-View-Controller.

answer.oneLiner: MVC is a way of organizing your app by splitting it into three roles so each one has a clear job. The Model holds your data and business logic, the View is what the user actually sees, and the Controller sits in the middle taking requests and coordinating the other two. A good comparison is a restaurant, where the kitchen prepares the food, the menu presents it, and the waiter ferries between them. You reach for this structure because keeping those concerns separate makes the code far easier to test and maintain.
keyPoints: When a request comes in, routing looks at the URL and figures out which controller action should handle it.
The controller then calls into the model, things like services or data access, and builds up a result from what comes back.
Next the controller hands a model object over to the view, and the view uses it to render the actual HTML.
Because each piece is kept separate, you can test the model, the controller, and the view independently of one another.

code.note: Controller is the coordinator; keep business logic in the Model/service layer

redFlags: Putting business logic in the controller or view ('fat controller').

dialogue.text: Explain the MVC architecture.
It separates concerns into Model (data and business logic), View (UI), and Controller (handles the request and coordinates the two) - which improves testability and separation.
Walk me through a request.
Routing maps the URL to a controller action. The controller calls the model/services, builds a result, and passes a model to the view, which renders HTML.
What's the classic anti-pattern?
A 'fat controller' - business logic in the controller or view. Logic belongs in the model/service layer so it's testable and reusable.

followUpsQA.a: The model/service layer, not the controller.

followUpsQA.q: How pass data to a view?

questions.q: Compare ViewBag, ViewData, TempData, and a ViewModel for passing data to a view.

answer.oneLiner: These are all ways to get data from a controller into a view, but they differ in safety and how long the data lives. A ViewModel is a proper strongly-typed class and it's the one you'd normally prefer. ViewData and ViewBag are loose dynamic containers that only last for the current request, and TempData is the special one that survives across a single redirect. So the choice really comes down to whether you want type safety and how far the data needs to travel.
keyPoints: A ViewModel is strongly typed, which means the compiler checks it and you get IntelliSense, so it's considered the best practice.
ViewData is just a dictionary you access with a string key like ViewData of key, and it only lives for the current request.
ViewBag is really just a dynamic wrapper sitting on top of ViewData, so it has the same lifetime but gives up type safety.
TempData is backed by session or a cookie, which is what lets it persist across one redirect.

code.snippet:
ViewData["Title"] = "Home";          // dictionary
ViewBag.Title = "Home";              // dynamic, same store
TempData["Msg"] = "Saved";           // survives redirect
return View(new ProductViewModel());  // strongly typed (preferred)
code.note: Prefer a ViewModel; use TempData for post-redirect flash messages

redFlags: Using ViewBag everywhere instead of strongly-typed ViewModels.

dialogue.text: Compare ViewBag, ViewData, TempData, and a ViewModel.
A ViewModel is a strongly-typed class - preferred. ViewData is a string-keyed dictionary, ViewBag is a dynamic wrapper over it, both for the current request. TempData survives one redirect.
Why prefer a ViewModel?
Compile-time checking, IntelliSense, and refactor safety - ViewBag/ViewData are dynamic and fail only at runtime.
When is TempData the right tool?
Post-redirect flash messages - like 'Saved successfully' after a redirect - since it persists across exactly one request.

followUpsQA.a: ViewBag is a dynamic wrapper over ViewData.

questions.q: What is a partial view and how do you pass data from it back to a controller?

answer.oneLiner: A partial view is a small reusable chunk of Razor markup in its own cshtml file that you drop inside other views, kind of like a building block you can reuse in several pages. The thing to understand is that a partial view by itself can't send data back to a controller. To get its data back, you put its input fields inside a form that posts to an action, and then the framework binds those posted values for you.
keyPoints: You render a partial using something like the partial tag helper with a name and model, or the older Html.Partial helper.
A partial view on its own doesn't post anything back; it's a form that actually does the posting.
So you place the form inputs inside the partial, and the surrounding form element is what posts to an action.
Once it posts, model binding takes those submitted fields and maps them onto the action's parameter.

code.note: Data flows back via form POST + model binding, not the partial itself

dialogue.text: What is a partial view and how does its data get back to a controller?
A partial view is a reusable Razor fragment rendered inside other views. It doesn't post by itself - you wrap its inputs in a form that POSTs to an action.
So how does the data actually flow back?
The surrounding `<form>` POSTs, and model binding maps the posted fields to the action's parameter.
How do you render a partial?
`<partial name="_Item" model="x" />` or the `Html.Partial` helper.

followUpsQA.q: Does a partial post by itself?

followUpsQA.a: The typed model for the form.

questions.q: What is the difference between a Razor View (MVC) and a Razor Page?

answer.oneLiner: The main difference is where the handling logic lives. With traditional MVC Razor views, a separate controller drives everything and one controller can serve many views. With Razor Pages, the page and its handler logic are bundled together, a cshtml file paired with its own cshtml.cs PageModel. That makes Razor Pages simpler and tidier for apps that are really organized around individual pages rather than shared controllers.
keyPoints: In MVC, controller actions return views, and a single controller often handles many different views.
With Razor Pages, each page gets its own PageModel that has handler methods like OnGet and OnPost.
Razor Pages cut down on the boilerplate for CRUD work and page-focused scenarios.
Both approaches use the exact same Razor syntax, and you can happily mix them in the same app.

code.note: Razor Pages suit page-based UIs; MVC suits APIs and complex routing

dialogue.text: Razor View (MVC) vs Razor Page?
MVC views are driven by separate controllers. Razor Pages bundle the page and its handler logic in a `.cshtml` plus `.cshtml.cs` PageModel - simpler for page-focused apps.
When would you pick Razor Pages?
Page-centric or CRUD scenarios - each page has its own OnGet/OnPost handlers, which cuts ceremony compared to a controller with many actions.
Can they coexist?
Yes - both use the same Razor syntax and can live in one app. MVC suits APIs and complex routing; Razor Pages suit page-based UIs.

questions.q: What are the common IActionResult return types in ASP.NET Core MVC?

answer.oneLiner: IActionResult is a flexible return type that lets a single action send back all sorts of responses, whether that's a rendered view, a redirect, some JSON, or just a status code. The action just describes what it wants to return, and the framework figures out how to resolve and execute it. It's handy because one method can return different kinds of results depending on what happens inside it.
keyPoints: Calling View returns a ViewResult, which renders a Razor page.
RedirectToAction or Redirect produce redirect responses with a 302 status.
Helpers like Ok, NotFound, and BadRequest give you plain status-code results, which is common in APIs.
Json returns JSON data, and File lets you send back a file download.

code.note: Return IActionResult when an action can return more than one result type

dialogue.text: What are the common IActionResult return types?
`View()` for a ViewResult, `RedirectToAction`/`Redirect` for redirects, `Ok`/`NotFound`/`BadRequest` for status codes, and `Json`/`File` for data or downloads.
Why return IActionResult instead of a concrete type?
So one action can return different result types - a view on validation failure, a redirect on success - resolved and executed by the framework.
API style example?
`return _svc.Find(id) is { } x ? Ok(x) : NotFound();` - 200 with the object or 404.

questions.q: Scenario: a date posted from a form makes the entire bound model null. What's going on?

answer.oneLiner: What's happening is that the date posted from the form is in a format the model binder can't parse, often a culture mismatch, so the binding for that field fails. When that happens the whole bound model can come back null, or you get a ModelState error, instead of a nicely parsed object. The classic culprit is one side expecting day-month order and the other expecting month-day. The fix is to make the date format unambiguous and to always check that binding actually succeeded.
keyPoints: Model binding parses dates according to the request's culture, so a dd/MM versus MM/dd mismatch will make it fail.
When a bind fails it adds an error to ModelState, which is why you should always check ModelState.IsValid before trusting the data.
The cleanest fix is to send dates in ISO 8601 format like yyyy-MM-dd, because that's the same regardless of culture.
You can also configure request localization, or use attributes like BindRequired or a custom binder, to take control of how parsing happens.

code.note: Use ISO date format from the client to dodge culture-specific parsing entirely

redFlags: Not checking ModelState.IsValid and assuming the model bound correctly.

dialogue.text: A posted date makes the whole bound model null. What's going on?
The date is in a format the request culture can't parse - `dd/MM` vs `MM/dd`. Binding fails and you get a ModelState error or a null model rather than the parsed object.
How do you confirm and handle it?
Always check `ModelState.IsValid` - a failed bind adds the error there instead of silently succeeding.
What's the robust fix?
Send ISO 8601 (`yyyy-MM-dd`) from the client - it's culture-invariant - or configure request localization / a custom binder.

followUpsQA.q: How to detect a bad bind?
